title: Small-n Approaches to Impact Evaluation
text:
Preface
This working paper presents a structured framework for conducting impact evaluations when sample sizes are small. It is addressed to evaluation commissioners, methodologists, and senior practitioners working in international development.
The paper originated from a recognition that existing methodological guidance on small-n evaluation, while valuable, remains fragmented across traditions and has not kept pace with methodological advances since 2012. The aim is not to replace earlier guides but to build on them: to organise the full repertoire of small-n methods under a common epistemological architecture and to extend coverage to methods that have matured or emerged since the last systematic treatments.
Readers already familiar with specific methods (Qualitative Comparative Analysis, process tracing, single-case experimental designs) will find them situated within a broader causal inference framework. Readers approaching small-n evaluation for the first time will find a principled basis for selecting and combining methods.
The paper is situated within the broader agenda of evidence-informed policymaking (EIPM). When programme scale precludes conventional evaluation designs, the evidence that commissioners and policymakers need simply does not get produced. A conceptual framework for EIPM (Sempé et al., 2025) identifies capabilities, relationships, structures, and evidence culture as the pathways through which evidence influences policy. This paper addresses the capabilities pathway directly: it expands the methodological repertoire available to evaluators working in small-n settings, so that credible causal evidence can be generated where it is currently under-supplied.




Sempé, L., Pande, R., Kelly, L., Anwar, A., Echt, L., & Jessani, N. (2025). A conceptual framework for evidence-informed policymaking [Framework narrative]. Foreign, Commonwealth; Development Office Research Commissioning Centre / International Initiative for Impact Evaluation (3ie). https://www.grtd.fcdo.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/RCC_EIPM_Framework_narrative.pdf

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 Contribution, scope, and structure
Many development programmes operate at a scale that places them beyond the reach of standard impact evaluation methods. National policy reforms may affect one country, institutional capacity-building programmes may serve a single ministry, and sector-wide approaches may span only three or four provinces (OECD-DAC Network on Development Evaluation, 2019; White & Phillips, 2012). Fragile-state interventions are often implemented in contexts so specific that meaningful comparators barely exist (Bamberger et al., 2012). In each of these settings, the number of units available for causal inference falls below the thresholds that large-sample techniques require for valid estimation and testing.
Large-n counterfactual designs dominate the evidence architecture of development (International Initiative for Impact Evaluation, 2020; White & Phillips, 2012). Randomised controlled trials as well as many quasi-experimental designs such as difference-in-differences estimators, instrumental variables, matching and regression discontinuity designs all depend on assumptions that fail or become untestable as the number of units shrinks. Asymptotic properties that underwrite standard inference cease to apply. Conventional significance thresholds become uninformative when the sampling distribution of the test statistic cannot be reliably estimated. The result is a mismatch between the demand for rigorous causal evidence and the methods that are widely available and promoted.
The mismatch matters because the demand for rigorous causal evidence in small-n settings is growing, not receding. The revised OECD-DAC evaluation criteria call for assessments of impact and coherence across all programme types, including those operating at local scale (OECD-DAC Network on Development Evaluation, 2019). The Sustainable Development Goals themselves generate evaluation questions at the level of individual countries, sectors, and institutions, where large-n designs are rarely feasible. Evaluators and commissioners need methods that produce credible causal claims when the number of treated units, comparison cases, or time points is small. Those methods exist, but the guidance on how to select, apply, and combine them has not kept pace with their development.
The gap is consequential for evidence-informed policymaking (EIPM). A conceptual framework for the evidence-to-policy ecosystem (Sempé et al., 2025) identifies four pathways through which evidence influences policy: capabilities of evidence producers and users, relationships and networks among actors, institutional structures and processes, and evidence culture. When evaluators lack methods suited to a programme’s scale, the bottleneck sits at the production stage of the ecosystem; no amount of knowledge brokering or institutional reform can compensate for evidence that was never generated. This paper addresses that supply-side constraint by expanding the methodological toolkit and providing a structured framework for its application.
A small number of documents have attempted to map the field of small-n evaluation methods systematically. Leeuw & Vaessen (2009) produced an early overview for the Network of Networks on Impact Evaluation (NONIE), setting out a broad typology of evaluation approaches that accommodated non-experimental designs without focusing exclusively on the small-n case. White & Phillips (2012) then produced the first treatment dedicated to small-n impact evaluation at 3ie, organising methods into two broad groups: those that construct a counterfactual and those that trace causal mechanisms. The paper introduced a useful conceptual distinction, though its coverage naturally reflected the methods available at the time, and the two-group classification left room for finer epistemological differentiation. Department for International Development (2012) argued for methodological pluralism and provided a broader mapping of designs and methods, but did not develop a systematic framework for choosing among small-n methods specifically. Vaessen et al. (2020) assembled an extensive sourcebook drawing on the World Bank’s Independent Evaluation Group, yet the book is not organised around the small-n problem; the reader must extract the relevant subset without the benefit of a unifying framework. Fox et al. (2022) produced the most detailed guide to date, presenting method profiles with explicit attention to sample size constraints and analytical procedures.
Publication trends confirm that the repertoire has shifted since these guides were written. Between 2012 and 2024, annual publication counts for several small-n methods increased substantially (Figure 1.1): qualitative comparative analysis grew from 236 to over 2,000 publications per year, realist evaluation from 183 to over 2,000, and single-case experimental designs from 109 to over 470 (OpenAlex, 2026). Methods that were marginal or non-existent in the guides, such as coincidence analysis, outcome harvesting, and Bayesian process tracing, now have active research communities. Any guide written today must account for these developments.
Yet growth in the academic literature has not translated into routine use. An evidence gap map of 5,327 empirical studies in fragile and conflict-affected settings (FCAS), contexts where small samples are the norm rather than the exception, found that specialised small-n causal inference methods remain rare (Sempé, 2026). Among all studies, only 38 mentioned Bayesian methods, 15 used process tracing, 7 applied realist evaluation, 6 employed system dynamics, 2 used agent-based modelling, and 1 each reported QCA and contribution analysis. Most Significant Change and outcome harvesting appeared in none. Even among the 1,154 studies with 15 or fewer units, the dominant analytical techniques were conventional approaches such as difference-in-differences, propensity score methods, and thematic analysis; the causal inference methods designed specifically for small samples were virtually absent. The gap between the methods that exist and the methods that practitioners actually deploy is one motivation for the decision framework and integration protocols presented in Part III.
This paper builds on the foundations laid by these earlier guides, particularly the counterfactual-versus-mechanism distinction introduced by White & Phillips (2012) and the methodological pluralism advocated by Department for International Development (2012). It extends their contributions in four directions. The first is an organising framework of eight causal inference logics, developed in Chapter 3. Rather than treating small-n methods as a flat list, the framework identifies eight distinct logics that small-n methods operationalise: regularity, counterfactual, multiple causation, manipulation, generative, participatory, simulated, and diagnostic. Each logic provides a different warrant for causal claims. Organising methods by the logic they invoke clarifies what each method can and cannot establish. It also reveals that many methods combine two or more logics, a property that existing guides treat as incidental rather than systematic.
The second contribution is coverage of methods that post-date from earlier guides. Chapter 4 treats single-case experimental designs and N-of-1 trials as rigorous counterfactual methods for small samples, including randomisation inference as an inferential tool. Chapter 5 introduces coincidence analysis alongside qualitative comparative analysis. Chapter 8 adds outcome harvesting to the participatory toolkit. Chapter 9 presents agent-based modelling and system dynamics as computational complements to small-n designs.
The third is a formal decision framework (Chapter 10). Existing guides advise evaluators to “consider” various factors when selecting a method but do not provide a structured tool for doing so. The decision framework crosses evaluation questions, programme attributes, and data constraints to produce a recommended set of causal logics and candidate methods. The fourth is a set of cross-logic integration protocols (Chapter 11) that specify how evidence from one causal logic can strengthen, qualify, or challenge claims produced under another.
The focus is impact evaluation understood as causal attribution or causal contribution: warranted claims about whether, how, or why an intervention caused observed outcomes. “Small n” is defined formally in Chapter 2 as a multi-dimensional condition encompassing few programme units, few participants, few comparators, and few time points. Where a method requires conditions available only at larger scale, the paper acknowledges this boundary rather than forcing a small-n interpretation. Synthetic control methods, which require a sizeable donor pool, are one such case. Examples throughout the paper are drawn from governance, health, education, agriculture, infrastructure, and fragile-state programming. The methods themselves are domain-general; the practical guidance and decision framework are calibrated to constraints that development evaluators typically face.
The paper is organised in four parts. Chapter 2 and Chapter 3 establish the conceptual foundations. Part II (Chapters 4 to 9) presents methods grouped by causal logic, each chapter opening with the epistemological warrant that the relevant logic provides. Part III addresses integration and practice: Chapter 10 provides the decision matrix, Chapter 11 presents combination protocols, and Chapter 12 discusses bias, transparency, and reporting standards. The concluding chapter (Chapter 13) synthesises the argument and identifies priorities for methodological development.

title: 2  Defining Small-n
text:
2.1 Boundary methods
“Small n” is not a single condition. The concept used in the evaluation literature covers at least four distinct situations, each posing different constraints on causal inference and calling for different methodological responses. White & Phillips (2012) introduced a typology of small-n settings; the present chapter refines and extends it, drawing on the subsequent literature and on the practical realities of development evaluation.
The first situation involves few programme units. A governance reform may target a single ministry. A decentralisation programme may operate across three provinces. A health system strengthening intervention may cover five districts within one region. In each case, the unit that received the intervention exists in small numbers, and the evaluator cannot increase the sample by changing the design. The indicative range is one to five treated units, though the precise threshold depends on what the evaluator needs to estimate.
The second situation involves few participants within a unit. A judicial capacity-building programme may train 15 judges. A leadership development initiative may enrol 20 school headteachers. A specialised agricultural extension scheme may work with 25 farmers selected for specific agroecological conditions. The programme reaches a bounded population, sometimes a census, and the evaluator works with all of them rather than sampling from a larger pool. Indicative thresholds vary by method, but most standard statistical techniques lose reliability below 30 observations, and many development programmes operate well below this level.
The third situation involves thin comparison groups. A quasi-experimental design may require matched comparators, but only 12 districts share the relevant baseline characteristics. A regression discontinuity design may have few observations near the threshold. A difference-in-differences estimator may have adequate treated units but struggle to identify untreated units with parallel pre-treatment trends. In these cases, the treated sample may not itself be small, but the effective sample for causal identification is.
The fourth situation involves few time points. Annual outcome data may span only three or four waves. A monitoring system may have been introduced two years before the evaluation. Seasonal data collection may yield six observations. Methods that rely on temporal variation, such as interrupted time series, require long panels to separate intervention effects from secular trends. When the temporal dimension is short, even a single-unit design faces additional constraints beyond the cross-sectional sample size.
These four dimensions are independent. A programme may be small on one dimension and adequate on another. A national reform (one treated unit) may generate rich within-case data from hundreds of administrative records. A training programme with 20 participants may have 50 repeated measurements per person. The methodological implications differ accordingly, and the choice of evaluation method depends on which dimension is constrained.
The small-n condition is not distributed randomly across the development portfolio. It clusters in precisely the settings where the demand for evidence is highest and the capacity to produce it is weakest. Fragile and conflict-affected states, institutional reform programmes, and policy pilot interventions all combine small-n constraints with the political instability, resource limitations, and weak institutional capacity that the EIPM framework identifies as moderating factors in the evidence-to-policy process (Sempé et al., 2025). When these moderating factors coincide with the absence of appropriate evaluation methods, the result is a compounding gap: evidence is hardest to generate in the contexts where it is hardest to use. Recognising which dimension of small n is binding is therefore not only a methodological exercise but a prerequisite for producing evidence that can enter the policy ecosystem at all.
Table 2.1 summarises the typology with indicative thresholds and examples from development evaluation.
The statistical consequences of small n are well established. Statistical power declines as the sample shrinks, and the probability of detecting a real effect falls to levels that make null results uninformative. Asymptotic properties that justify standard test statistics and confidence intervals cease to hold. The sampling distribution of the test statistic cannot be reliably estimated, so conventional significance thresholds (p < 0.05) lose their intended meaning (White & Phillips, 2012). The distinction between design-based and model-based inference becomes consequential: design-based inference, which derives its validity from randomisation rather than from distributional assumptions, can remain valid at very small n if the randomisation structure is known (see Chapter 4 on randomisation inference). Model-based inference, by contrast, depends on parametric assumptions that are difficult to verify with few observations.
These statistical limitations constrain what can be detected, not what exists. The insight is older than modern statistics. Aristotle held that causation is a real feature of the natural world: things have causal powers by virtue of what they are, and understanding a phenomenon means identifying the causes that produced it (Aristotle, 1930). This realist view prevailed for two millennia, until Hume (1748) argued that we never observe causation directly but only regular conjunction between events, and that causal claims are therefore always inferences from repeated experience. Much of modern statistical methodology inherits the Humean framework: causal effects are defined as differences across many observations, and small samples are treated as obstacles to causal knowledge because they provide too few conjunctions to ground a reliable inference. Bhaskar (2008) recovered the Aristotelian position within a modern philosophy of science, arguing that causal powers are real properties of structures and mechanisms, whether or not anyone observes them. Cartwright (2007) argued that no single method holds a monopoly on causal knowledge, and that different inferential strategies capture different aspects of the causal relationship. Pawson & Tilley (1997) grounded this insight in evaluation practice, showing that causal mechanisms operate in the real world and that the evaluator’s task is to uncover them, not to create them through design. Recognising the distinction between the existence of a causal effect and the ability to demonstrate it through a particular statistical framework is essential, because it opens the door to alternative modes of causal reasoning rather than treating small n as a dead end.
When n is small, each case carries rich, detailed information. A single ministry undergoing reform generates documents, key informant perspectives, process data, and contextual knowledge that no large-n study could gather across hundreds of units. This density of within-case evidence creates opportunities for causal inference strategies that do not depend on cross-case comparison at all.
Holland (1986) distinguished between “effects of causes” (what is the average effect of treatment X?) and “causes of effects” (what caused outcome Y in this particular case?). Large-n methods are designed for the first question. Small-n methods can address both, and some exist precisely for the second (Fox et al., 2022). The two questions are complementary, and an evaluator facing a small-n setting is not limited to a weaker form of inquiry; rather, the form of inquiry changes in kind.
Several evaluation methods appear to operate at small n on one dimension but require large n on another. These boundary cases help clarify the scope of this paper.
Synthetic control methods reconstruct a counterfactual for a single treated unit by constructing a weighted combination of untreated units (Abadie, 2021). The treated unit is n = 1, but the method requires a donor pool of 20 to 50 or more untreated units observed over a long pre-treatment panel. The synthetic control method solves a “few treated units” problem, not a “few total units” problem. It is an important method for policy evaluation, but its data requirements place it outside the core scope of the small-n toolkit as defined here.
Econometric time-series methods such as interrupted time series and ARIMA models can analyse a single unit, but they substitute temporal n for cross-sectional n. A reliable interrupted time series analysis typically requires 50 or more time points to model the counterfactual trend with confidence. When the temporal dimension is short, these methods face the same fundamental constraints as cross-sectional designs with few units.
Microsimulation models individual-level transitions through policy rules and can operate at the level of a single policy reform. However, the method typically requires large representative micro-datasets to populate the simulation. The unit of policy interest may be singular, but the data demands are large-n in character.
These boundary cases are not excluded because they lack rigour. They are treated separately because the constraints they address, and the data they require, differ from the situations described in Table 2.1. Table 2.2 summarises the distinction.

title: 2  Defining Small-n
text:
2.2 Constraint and choice
Small-n evaluation arises in two distinct ways, and the distinction matters for how evaluators and commissioners think about method selection.
In many cases, small n is a constraint. The programme was implemented in three districts. The training reached 12 participants. The outcome data span four years. No design decision can change these facts, and the evaluator must select methods that produce valid inferences within the available sample. This is the situation most evaluation guidance addresses, and it is the most common entry point for the methods described in this paper.
In other cases, small n is a deliberate choice. The evaluator could, in principle, conduct a large-n study, but the evaluation question requires a different form of evidence. A commissioner who needs to understand how a reform produced its effects in a specific institutional context will learn more from a within-case process tracing study than from a cross-site average treatment effect. An evaluation of a complex programme operating through multiple causal pathways may require configurational analysis to identify which combinations of conditions produced the outcome, a question that averaging across cases would obscure. A programme intended for scale-up may need explanatory evidence about context-mechanism-outcome interactions, not just an effect size that may not transfer.
The evaluation question itself determines whether a small-n design is appropriate. Table 2.3 maps four common types of evaluation question to the reasoning that supports a small-n approach.



Table 2.3: Evaluation questions and the rationale for small-n approaches










Evaluation question
Why small-n methods may be appropriate
Example




Did the intervention produce the outcome?
When few units exist, within-case or configurational comparison replaces cross-case comparison
A single ministry reform; a 4-district pilot


How did the intervention produce its effects?
Mechanistic explanation requires within-case depth, not cross-case breadth
Process tracing of a policy adoption pathway

Configurational methods identify sufficient and necessary conditions across cases

For whom did the intervention work, and under what conditions?
Participatory methods capture heterogeneity that averages conceal

The implication is that small-n methods are not a second-best option reserved for situations where large-n designs are infeasible. They are a first-choice option when the evaluation question demands causal explanation, configurational analysis, or within-case depth. The decision framework in Chapter 10 formalises this reasoning. The next chapter identifies the causal inference logics available when n is small, which is the foundation on which the method chapters in Part II are built.

title: 3  The Eight Logics of Causal Inference
text:
3.1 Why multiple logics?
Chapter 2 established that small n constrains what can be detected statistically but does not alter whether a causal relationship exists. It traced a line from Aristotle’s realism through Hume’s scepticism to Bhaskar’s recovery of causal powers as real features of the world. This chapter extends that story into the modern formalisms that evaluators actually use, and then identifies distinct logics through which causal claims can be warranted when n is small. These logics are the organising spine of the book: every method presented in Part II is an operationalisation of one or more of them.
Four traditions of causal reasoning shape how evaluators think about cause and effect, and each privileges a different form of evidence. Mill (1843) proposed eliminative canons (including the methods of agreement, difference, residues, and concomitant variation) through which an investigator compares cases systematically to isolate the factor that makes the difference. Mill’s logic requires no statistical apparatus; it operates through structured comparison across as few as two cases. Mackie (1965) introduced the INUS condition: a cause is an insufficient but necessary part of an unnecessary but sufficient condition. This formulation recognised that most real-world outcomes arise from conjunctions of factors rather than single causes. A development programme may be necessary within a particular causal package (alongside political will, institutional capacity, and adequate funding) without being sufficient on its own. Rubin (1974) extended and formalised the potential outcomes framework, originally proposed by Neyman (1990) for randomised experiments, to observational settings. In this framework, the effect of a treatment on a unit is the difference between the outcome under treatment and the outcome under control, only one of which is ever observed. The Rubin framework gives precise meaning to “the counterfactual” but requires either randomisation or strong assumptions to estimate the missing potential outcome across units. Pearl (2009) recast causation in terms of structural equations and directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), defining a causal effect as the consequence of an intervention (the do-operator) on a variable within a specified graph. Pearl’s framework provides a formal language for identifying what must be conditioned on to estimate causal effects, but the identification strategies it formalises (the back-door and front-door criteria, and graphical conditions for valid instrumental variable estimation) generally require large samples.
These four traditions do not compete; they answer different questions. Table 3.1 compares them on four dimensions that matter for evaluation practice.
The mapping from traditions to evaluation methods is not one-to-one. Mill’s eliminative logic appears in qualitative comparative analysis. Mackie’s INUS conditions underpin both QCA and coincidence analysis. Rubin’s potential outcomes framework grounds single-case experimental designs, where the unit serves as its own control across phases. Pearl’s DAGs serve a cross-cutting role: regardless of which causal logic an evaluator invokes, a DAG can make the assumed causal structure explicit, clarify which confounders are assumed absent, and identify which variables need to be observed. This representational function is distinct from Pearl’s estimation strategies and remains valuable at any sample size. The decision framework in Chapter 10 revisits this cross-cutting role.
The different logics arise from recognising that these four traditions, together with subsequent developments in evaluation theory, generate eight distinguishable ways of warranting a causal claim. Each logic relies on a different type of evidence, asks a different canonical question, and maps onto a different family of evaluation methods. Table 3.2 presents the full architecture.
The most common objection to a multi-logic framework is that the counterfactual logic is the gold standard and other logics represent weaker alternatives to be used only when counterfactual designs are infeasible (Deaton & Cartwright, 2018). This view conflates one tradition’s definition of causation with causation itself. The counterfactual logic asks “what would have happened without the intervention?”, a precise and valuable question, but not the only question that matters for evaluation. It does not ask how the intervention produced its effects, whether the same combination of conditions would produce the effect elsewhere, or why the intervention worked in one site but not another. These are distinct causal questions, not inferior versions of the counterfactual question, and they require distinct logics to answer.
Cartwright (2007) argued that different methods of causal inference do not merely offer alternative routes to the same causal conclusion; they capture different causal concepts (causes as difference-makers, causes as capacities, causes as INUS conditions) and the appropriate method depends on which causal question is being asked. Mackie (1965) argued that what we ordinarily call “a cause” is an INUS condition: an insufficient but necessary part of a package of conditions that is jointly sufficient for the outcome. On this analysis, causes operate within conjunctions rather than as isolated difference-makers. Development interventions are almost always contributory causes in this sense. A conditional cash transfer programme may increase school enrolment, but only in conjunction with accessible schools, functioning payment systems, and household decision-making norms that permit girls to attend. The counterfactual logic asks whether the outcome would have been different without the programme; the multiple-causation logic (Logic 3) identifies which combinations of conditions produced the outcome; the generative logic (Logic 5) traces the mechanism through which the transfer changed household behaviour. Each adds causal knowledge that the others cannot supply.
Scriven (2008) challenged the assumption that any single method could anchor all causal inference, arguing that causal claims can be warranted through systematic elimination of rival explanations without experimental manipulation. Patton (2008) made the practical case that evaluation design must flow from the questions of intended users: a finance ministry asking “did this reform reduce procurement fraud?” needs counterfactual or diagnostic evidence, a programme manager asking “why did the reform work in three provinces but not in two?” needs configurational evidence, and a civil society organisation asking “how did affected communities experience the reform?” needs participatory evidence. The logics are not ranked; they are matched to purpose.

title: 3  The Eight Logics of Causal Inference
text: The CCT example is well documented. Schultz (2004) evaluates Mexico’s Progresa programme, showing how transfers interacted with school supply and household norms to increase enrolment. Fiszbein & Schady (2009) synthesise evidence across countries, confirming that CCT effects depend on conjunctions of conditions rather than the transfer alone.

title: 3  The Eight Logics of Causal Inference
text:
3.2 Logics at small n
Six of the eight logics have dedicated method families that operate at small n, and these form the substance of Part II. The counterfactual logic (Chapter 4) grounds single-case experimental designs and N-of-1 trials, where within-unit temporal comparison replaces between-group comparison. The multiple causation logic (Chapter 5) grounds qualitative comparative analysis, coincidence analysis, and other configurational methods designed for samples of 10 to 50 cases. The generative logic (Chapter 6) grounds process tracing, contribution analysis, and realist evaluation, which build causal claims from mechanistic evidence: process tracing works within individual cases, contribution analysis traces causal pathways through a theory of change drawing on multiple evidence sources, and realist evaluation compares context-mechanism-outcome configurations across sites. The participatory logic (Chapter 8) grounds most significant change and outcome harvesting, which generate causal narratives from the accounts of social actors and programme stakeholders, supplemented by documentary evidence and independent verification. The simulated logic (Chapter 9) grounds agent-based modelling and system dynamics, which serve three complementary roles: exploring intervention logic and emergent dynamics, generating prior estimates for Bayesian methods, and constructing computational counterfactuals. The diagnostic logic (Chapter 7) grounds diagnostic evaluation approaches, including Bayesian process tracing and simulated probability estimation, where each piece of evidence updates the probability that a hypothesised causal relationship holds.
Two logics receive briefer treatment because their small-n applicability, though real, is narrower.
The regularity logic (Logic 1) warrants causal claims through consistent co-occurrence across observations, detected through statistical methods. The logic traces directly to Hume’s account of causation as regular conjunction: if X is consistently followed by Y, and the association survives controls for confounders, the analyst infers that X causes Y. At large n, this is the bread and butter of econometric causal inference. At small n, regularity reasoning requires substituting temporal observations for cross-sectional ones: a single unit observed across 50 or more time points can support interrupted time series analysis, where the regularity is between the policy change and a break in the outcome trend. Bernal et al. (2017) provide a comprehensive treatment of interrupted time series methods, noting that the approach requires sufficient pre-intervention data points (typically 20 to 50) to model the counterfactual trend with confidence. When fewer time points are available, the regularity logic loses its inferential power because the analyst cannot distinguish a genuine break from random fluctuation. The temporal n requirement places interrupted time series at the boundary of the small-n toolkit (see Section 2.1), but the logic itself is present whenever an evaluator argues “the outcome changed when and only when the intervention was introduced.”
The manipulation logic (Logic 4), rooted in the interventionist theory of causation (Woodward, 2003), warrants causal claims through the act of deliberate intervention: the evaluator or programme manager changes X and observes whether Y follows. Woodward (2003) argues that a variable X is a cause of Y if and only if there exists an intervention on X that would change Y, holding all other relevant variables fixed. At small n, this logic is most visible in SCED phase changes, where the investigator introduces and withdraws the treatment in a controlled sequence. The manipulation logic is also implicit in adaptive management approaches, where programme managers deliberately vary implementation features across cycles and observe the consequences. Because SCED designs combine manipulation with counterfactual reasoning through within-unit comparison, the manipulation logic is treated as embedded within the counterfactual chapter (Chapter 4) rather than separately. The distinction matters conceptually: the counterfactual logic asks “what would have happened without X?” while the manipulation logic asks “what happens when we do X?” In SCEDs, both questions are answered simultaneously through controlled phase changes.
Several methods invoke more than one logic simultaneously, and these combinations provide stronger causal warrants than any single logic alone. Bayesian process tracing combines diagnostic logic (updating prior probabilities) with generative logic (tracing the mechanism that links cause to effect). Realist evaluation combines generative logic (context-mechanism-outcome configurations) with structured cross-case comparison, asking which contexts trigger which mechanisms to produce which outcomes across sites. Outcome harvesting combines participatory logic (social actors identify changes and contribution claims) with structured corroboration, in which independent reviewers verify each claim against documentary and third-party evidence. Figure 3.1 displays the eight logics and the connections between them. The integration protocols in Chapter 11 formalise how evaluators can combine logics within a single evaluation design.






Figure 3.1: The eight logics of causal inference. Line thickness is proportional to co-occurrence in the evaluation literature (OpenAlex, 2000–2025). Grey nodes indicate logics with narrower small-n applicability, treated briefly in this chapter rather than in dedicated Part II chapters.



The next chapter begins Part II by examining the first method family: counterfactual designs for small samples.

title: 4  Counterfactual Designs for Small Samples
text:
4.1 Single-case experimental designs and N-of-1 trials
The counterfactual question does not disappear when n is small. “What would have happened without the intervention?” remains the right question whenever the evaluation needs to isolate a treatment effect. What changes is the strategy for constructing the counterfactual. Large-sample designs compare a treated group with a control group, relying on randomisation or statistical adjustment to make the groups exchangeable. When the sample contains only one unit, or a handful, between-group comparison cannot support credible inference. The primary alternative is within-unit temporal comparison: each case serves as its own control across phases of treatment and non-treatment. This is the domain of single-case experimental designs (SCEDs) and N-of-1 trials.
Within-unit comparison does not require large-sample distributional assumptions. Randomisation inference, an inferential overlay discussed in the second section of this chapter, provides exact p-values by enumerating all possible phase assignments. The result is a family of designs that can produce internally valid causal evidence from as few as one unit, provided the evaluator can observe the outcome repeatedly across phases. Chapter 2 identified boundary methods (synthetic control, interrupted time series) that also invoke counterfactual logic but require larger temporal n. This chapter focuses on designs that operate at genuinely small n and that give each unit its own counterfactual.
SCEDs are a family of experimental designs in which each unit is observed across multiple phases, and the investigator controls when the treatment is introduced, withdrawn, or alternated (Epstein & Dallery, 2022). The counterfactual is constructed by comparing the same unit’s outcome between treatment and non-treatment phases. Because the unit serves as its own control, between-subject variability is eliminated. The family contains four principal design types, each suited to different assumptions about the outcome and the intervention. Table 4.1 summarises the key features.
Reversal designs (ABA or ABAB) introduce the treatment in phase B and withdraw it to return to baseline in a subsequent A phase (Barlow et al., 2009). The counterfactual for the treatment phase is the observed baseline; the counterfactual for the return-to-baseline phase is the preceding treatment phase. The design assumes that the outcome is reversible: if the treatment effect persists after withdrawal, the return-to-baseline phase will not show a change, and the design cannot distinguish a lasting treatment effect from no effect. Reversal designs are strongest when the outcome fluctuates with the treatment, as in behavioural interventions or dosage studies.
Multiple baseline designs stagger the introduction of the treatment across at least three units, settings, or behaviours (Kratochwill et al., 2013). Each baseline that has not yet received the treatment serves as a concurrent control for the unit that has. The design does not require reversibility, which makes it suitable for interventions whose effects are expected to persist, such as training, education, or infrastructure improvements. The staggered introduction rules out maturation and history threats: if the outcome changes only when the treatment is introduced, and this pattern repeats across tiers, the inference that the treatment caused the change is strengthened.
Alternating treatments designs rapidly switch between two or more conditions within the same unit, typically within a single session or across consecutive sessions (Barlow et al., 2009). The design compares conditions concurrently rather than sequentially, making it useful for comparing the relative effectiveness of two treatments. Combined designs merge elements from the above families. Epstein & Dallery (2022) describes combined designs as the most flexible but also the most complex to analyse, because the inferential logic depends on which components are present.
N-of-1 trials are a subtype of SCEDs rather than a separate family (Epstein & Dallery, 2022). They are personalised crossover trials in which a single participant receives treatment and control conditions in a randomised sequence, often with blinding (Guyatt et al., 1986). N-of-1 trials are most common in clinical medicine, where the goal is to determine whether a specific treatment works for a specific patient. The randomised phase order and blinding distinguish N-of-1 trials from standard reversal designs, but the underlying logic is the same: within-unit temporal comparison.
SCEDs build external validity through replication rather than through sampling. Within-case replication repeats phases within a single unit: an ABAB design replicates the treatment effect twice within the same case. Across-case replication repeats the entire design with new units. Tanious et al. (2024) emphasise that both forms of replication are necessary for credible causal inference. Without within-case replication, a single phase change could reflect coincidence. Without across-case replication, the finding may be idiosyncratic to one unit.
Randomisation within SCEDs strengthens the inferential basis. Tanious et al. (2024) describe two main strategies: randomising the onset of the treatment phase (common in multiple baseline designs) and randomising the sequence of conditions (common in alternating treatments designs). Blinding participants and assessors to phase assignment reduces expectancy and measurement bias. Randomisation also enables randomisation inference, discussed below, by providing the reference distribution against which the observed effect is tested.
Development evaluation has not yet adopted SCEDs widely, but several application domains are natural fits. Personalised behaviour-change interventions in health and nutrition programmes can use reversal or N-of-1 designs when the outcome is a repeated behaviour (adherence, dietary intake, hand-washing frequency). Adaptive management in fragile settings, where a single school, clinic, or district is the unit of interest, can use multiple baseline designs to stagger the introduction of a procedural change across sites. Treatment-development studies, in which a programme is being piloted before scaling, can use SCEDs to determine whether the intervention produces a detectable effect before investing in a large-sample trial.

Development evaluation application: writing intervention in Spanish primary schools



Arrimada et al. (2018) evaluated a Response to Intervention (RTI) writing programme for twelve first-grade students scoring below the 15th percentile in composition in Spain. Each student was measured on handwriting quality and composition through twice-weekly probe tasks across approximately 192 days. A multiple-baseline design staggered the introduction of parent-delivered writing instruction across students: baseline measurement continued for 120 days of regular classroom instruction, followed by 72 days of intervention. The staggered introduction ruled out maturation and history as rival explanations. Ten of twelve students improved in composition quality, with eight reaching the normal range. The study illustrates how a multiple-baseline SCED can produce credible causal evidence for an educational intervention operating at genuinely small n, without requiring a control group drawn from a large population.

title: 4  Counterfactual Designs for Small Samples
text:
4.2 Analytical tools for within-unit designs
Visual inspection is the traditional analytical approach in SCED research. The analyst examines graphed data for changes in level, trend, and variability between phases. Visual inspection is transparent and requires no distributional assumptions. Its principal limitations are well documented: inter-rater agreement on whether a phase change is “meaningful” is often low; visual inspection is insensitive to small or gradual effects; and it provides no formal uncertainty quantification. Visual inspection remains a useful first step, but it is insufficient as the sole basis for causal claims.
Linear mixed-effects modelling (LMEM) offers a statistical alternative that handles the repeated-measures structure of SCED data. Wiley & Rapp (2019) demonstrate LMEM with data from a longitudinal training study of five individuals with acquired dysgraphia. The model specifies fixed effects for the treatment phase (capturing the average treatment effect) and random effects for individual variation (capturing how the effect differs across units). LMEM accommodates both binary outcomes (accuracy) and continuous outcomes (reaction time), handles unequal phase lengths, and accounts for autocorrelated errors. The key advantage is that LMEM produces standardised effect sizes and formal tests of statistical significance even with very few individuals. Wiley & Rapp (2019) argue that LMEM is preferable to visual inspection or simpler statistical techniques when the goal is to quantify the treatment effect.
Beyond LMEM, several effect size metrics have been developed specifically for SCED data. Non-overlap of all pairs (NAP) calculates the proportion of treatment-phase data points that exceed all baseline-phase data points, yielding a non-parametric measure of effect magnitude. Tau-U extends NAP by correcting for baseline trend before computing the overlap statistic. The between-case standardised mean difference provides a metric that is comparable to the Cohen’s d used in group designs, enabling cross-study comparison and meta-analysis. These metrics communicate practical significance beyond what a p-value alone conveys. Table 4.2 summarises the main analytical options.

Randomisation inference provides an exact inferential framework for any SCED in which phase assignments have been randomised. The logic is straightforward. Under the sharp null hypothesis that the treatment has no effect on any observation, the observed sequence of outcomes would have occurred regardless of which time points were assigned to treatment and which to baseline. The analyst enumerates (or simulates by random sampling) all possible phase assignments permitted by the randomisation scheme, computes a test statistic for each, and locates the observed test statistic in that reference distribution. The p-value is the proportion of assignments that yield a test statistic as extreme as, or more extreme than, the one observed. No distributional assumptions are needed; the inference is exact.
The sharp null hypothesis (no effect for any observation) differs from the weak null hypothesis (no average effect), and the choice between them has consequences. Under the sharp null, any individual observation is assumed unaffected; under the weak null, effects may cancel out across observations. The sharp null is the more stringent test and the one typically used in randomisation inference for SCEDs. Fisher proposed this form of exact inference; Neyman’s framework focuses instead on average effects and confidence intervals. For SCED applications, Fisher’s sharp null is the natural choice because the goal is usually to determine whether the treatment affected the specific unit under study.
Randomisation inference is not a standalone method. It is an inferential overlay that strengthens any randomised small-n design. The randomised phase-change schedule provides the reference distribution; the test statistic can be any summary of the treatment-baseline contrast (mean difference, NAP, trend slope). The approach combines naturally with LMEM: the model provides the effect estimate and the randomisation distribution provides the p-value, avoiding reliance on asymptotic assumptions that may not hold at small n.
A concrete example illustrates the logic. Suppose a multiple-baseline design assigns treatment onset to three community health workers at randomly selected time points within a window of weeks 4 to 12. The randomisation scheme permits \(\binom{9}{3} = 84\) possible onset assignments. For each of the 84 possible assignments, the analyst computes the mean difference in performance between treatment and baseline phases. The observed mean difference is then compared against this reference distribution. If the observed difference exceeds 80 of the 84 possible values, the exact p-value is \(4/84 \approx 0.048\). No assumption about the shape of the outcome distribution is needed, because the inference is grounded entirely in the randomisation schedule that the evaluator controlled. Bulté & Onghena (2009) provide a comprehensive treatment of randomisation tests for single-case designs, including software implementations in the R package SCRT.

title: 4  Counterfactual Designs for Small Samples
section: 4.3 Embedding within-unit designs in larger studies
text:
4.3 Embedding within-unit designs in larger studies
Two strategies connect SCED evidence to broader evaluation frameworks. The first embeds N-of-1 trials within multi-site studies or randomised controlled trials. Each participant completes a personalised SCED, and the individual-level effect estimates feed into a group-level analysis. This design retains the within-unit causal logic while accumulating evidence across units. The second strategy meta-analyses across independently conducted SCEDs, synthesising effect sizes from separately published single-case studies using the between-case standardised mean difference or hierarchical models. Both strategies extend the reach of within-unit designs beyond the single case. Chapter 11 discusses integration protocols in detail.

The principal strengths of within-unit counterfactual designs are clear. They produce causal evidence at n as low as 1. Randomisation inference provides exact p-values without large-sample assumptions. Each unit serves as its own control, eliminating between-subject confounding. Internal validity for the treated unit is high.
The limitations are equally clear. All SCED variants require repeated measurement: many time points per unit per phase. Reversal designs assume that the outcome returns to baseline after treatment withdrawal, an assumption violated for interventions whose effects persist (learning, vaccination, infrastructure). Multiple baseline designs require at least three units, settings, or behaviours, and assume no spillover between tiers. External validity depends entirely on replication; a single SCED demonstrates an effect for one unit and cannot, on its own, support generalisation. Development evaluation has adopted SCEDs sparingly to date, in part because many development outcomes are not repeatedly measurable in the short cycles that SCED phases require.






The case against and for SCEDs



SCEDs have faced persistent scepticism, particularly from fields that treat group-randomised trials as the default standard of evidence. Three criticisms recur. First, findings from a single case cannot inform policy because they lack external validity. Second, the reversibility assumption restricts SCEDs to a narrow class of outcomes that return to baseline when the treatment is withdrawn, excluding most developmental, educational, or structural outcomes. Third, evidence hierarchies such as GRADE and the Campbell Collaboration have historically placed single-case evidence below group-level experimental evidence, limiting its influence on systematic reviews and policy guidance.
Proponents counter each objection. On external validity, Barlow et al. (2009) and Tanious et al. (2024) argue that SCEDs build generalisability through systematic replication across cases, settings, and behaviours rather than through sampling. A single SCED cannot generalise, but a series of replicated SCEDs can, and the replication logic is transparent: each new case either confirms or disconfirms the finding. On reversibility, multiple baseline and alternating treatments designs do not require the outcome to reverse, and the family as a whole is broader than the reversal design alone. On evidence standards, Kratochwill et al. (2013) established formal design standards for SCEDs within the What Works Clearinghouse, and meta-analytic techniques now allow SCED effect sizes to be synthesised alongside group-design effect sizes. Wiley & Rapp (2019) demonstrate that LMEM provides the same inferential outputs (effect sizes, confidence intervals, p-values) that reviewers expect from group trials. The debate is not settled, but the methodological infrastructure for treating SCED evidence on equal terms with group evidence has advanced considerably since the early critiques.


The counterfactual logic at small n trades breadth for depth. Within-unit designs produce strong internal validity for individual cases at the cost of generalisability. The integration strategies described above, and the multi-logic combinations discussed in Chapter 11, are how evaluators address this trade-off. The next chapter turns to a different causal logic, one that does not ask “what would have happened?” but rather “which combinations of conditions produced this outcome?”

title: 5  Configurational Approaches
text:
5.1 Qualitative comparative analysis
The previous chapter asked “what would have happened without the intervention?” and constructed the counterfactual through within-unit temporal comparison. This chapter asks a different question: “which combinations of conditions produced the outcome?” The multiple-causation logic recognises that development interventions rarely act alone. A conditional cash transfer programme may increase school enrolment, but only in conjunction with accessible schools, functioning payment systems, and household norms that permit girls to attend. The appropriate formalism is not the isolated difference-maker but the INUS condition introduced by Mackie (1965): an insufficient but necessary part of an unnecessary but sufficient conjunction. Configurational comparative methods (CCMs) operationalise this logic by searching for the combinations of conditions that are consistently associated with the outcome across a moderate number of cases.
Two principal methods dominate the CCM family. Qualitative comparative analysis (QCA), introduced by Ragin (1987), constructs truth tables from case-level data and applies logical minimisation to identify sufficient and necessary conditions for the outcome. Coincidence analysis (CNA), developed by Baumgartner (2009), takes a different approach: it searches the data for all minimally sufficient conditions without requiring truth-table construction or prior specification of which factor is the outcome. Both methods operationalise INUS logic, but they differ in analytical strategy, assumptions, and the role of the researcher. The choice between them has become a subject of active methodological debate.
QCA exists in three variants, each suited to different data structures. Crisp-set QCA (csQCA) codes all conditions as binary: present or absent. Fuzzy-set QCA (fsQCA) calibrates conditions to degrees of membership between 0 and 1, allowing graded distinctions (Ragin, 2008). Multi-value QCA (mvQCA) permits conditions to take more than two discrete values without requiring the continuous calibration that fsQCA demands. Table 5.1 summarises the three variants.
The analytical steps of a QCA study follow a consistent sequence regardless of variant. The analyst first identifies the conditions (potential causal factors) and the outcome, drawing on theory and case knowledge. Raw data are then calibrated into set memberships. For csQCA this means applying a threshold to classify each case as in or out of the set; for fsQCA it means specifying three qualitative anchors (full membership, crossover point, full non-membership) and using a logistic function to transform continuous data into fuzzy-set scores. The analyst constructs a truth table that lists all logically possible combinations of conditions alongside the number of observed cases and the outcome value for each row. Logical minimisation, typically using the Quine-McCluskey algorithm, reduces the truth table to the simplest set of configurations that account for the outcome. The Quine-McCluskey algorithm works by comparing pairs of configurations that differ on only one condition: if two configurations both produce the outcome and differ only on whether condition A is present, then A is logically redundant for that pair, and the algorithm removes it. This pairwise comparison is repeated until no further simplification is possible. The result takes three forms: a complex solution (no simplifying assumptions about unobserved configurations), a parsimonious solution (all logically possible simplifying assumptions), and an intermediate solution (only theoretically plausible simplifying assumptions). The complex solution is the most conservative: it reports only what the observed data support. The parsimonious solution is the most compact: it assumes that unobserved configurations would behave in whatever way produces the simplest formula. The intermediate solution occupies the middle ground: the analyst specifies which simplifying assumptions are theoretically plausible (for instance, “if political will is absent, the reform would not succeed regardless of other conditions”) and allows only those assumptions to simplify the solution. Schneider & Wagemann (2012) recommend reporting all three solutions and focusing interpretation on the intermediate solution, because it balances parsimony with theoretical defensibility.
Calibration deserves separate attention because it is the step at which substantive knowledge enters most directly. The three anchors in fsQCA are not determined by the data distribution; they are set by the analyst on the basis of theoretical or contextual knowledge about what constitutes “high” or “low” membership in a condition. A GDP-per-capita threshold for “high income” in a study of sub-Saharan African countries will differ from the same threshold in a study of OECD members. Poor calibration choices can shift cases across the crossover point and alter the truth table, changing the solution. This sensitivity is a feature of the method, not a defect: it forces the analyst to make substantive judgements explicit. But it also means that readers must be able to inspect and challenge the calibration decisions (Schneider & Wagemann, 2012).
Two types of set-theoretic relationship are central to QCA. A condition is necessary for the outcome if it is present in every case that shows the outcome. A configuration of conditions is sufficient for the outcome if every case with that configuration shows the outcome. Consistency measures the degree to which the empirical evidence supports the set-theoretic claim (perfect consistency = 1.0). Coverage measures the proportion of outcome cases that the configuration explains. Most QCA studies focus on sufficiency analysis, but necessity analysis is a distinct and important preliminary step: if a condition is necessary, it must appear in every sufficient configuration.
QCA is well suited to development evaluation questions that compare a moderate number of sites or countries. A multi-site evaluation of a governance reform implemented across 15 countries can use QCA to identify which combinations of contextual conditions (political competition, bureaucratic capacity, donor pressure, civil society strength) are associated with successful reform outcomes. A multi-component programme delivered across 20 schools can use QCA to determine which combinations of implementation features (teacher training completed, parental engagement achieved, materials delivered on time) are sufficient for improved learning outcomes. Software for QCA includes the standalone application fsQCA and the R packages QCA and SetMethods.

Development evaluation application: social accountability programmes across countries



Waddington et al. (2019) apply QCA to examine the conditions under which social accountability interventions improve public service delivery across low- and middle-income countries. The analysis covers 18 cases drawn from a systematic review of citizen engagement programmes, coding conditions such as programme design features (information provision, collective action facilitation), contextual factors (state capacity, civic space), and implementation quality. The fsQCA identifies two distinct pathways to improved service outcomes: one combining intensive information campaigns with organised citizen groups in contexts of moderate state responsiveness, and another combining direct engagement between citizens and service providers with strong programme implementation regardless of state capacity. The analysis reveals equifinality: the same outcome arises through different causal packages depending on context, a finding that a cross-case average treatment effect would have concealed.




Schneider & Wagemann (2006) apply QCA to the consolidation of democracy across post-communist states, identifying combinations of proximate and remote conditions — a canonical example of configurational analysis of governance reforms across a moderate number of countries.

title: 5  Configurational Approaches
text:
5.2 Coincidence analysis
CNA differs from QCA in three respects that have practical consequences. First, CNA does not require the analyst to specify which factor is the outcome before the analysis begins. It searches for sufficient and necessary conditions across all factors simultaneously, constructing causal models bottom-up from the data (Baumgartner, 2009). Second, CNA does not construct a truth table. It identifies redundancy-free Boolean dependency structures directly, using fit parameters (consistency and coverage, computed differently from QCA) to evaluate candidate solutions. Third, CNA can model multi-outcome structures in a single analysis: if condition A is sufficient for intermediate outcome B, and B combined with C is sufficient for final outcome D, CNA can recover the entire causal chain rather than treating each outcome separately.
The practical implication is that CNA is less reliant on the analyst’s prior specification of the causal structure. When the research question is exploratory (“what causes what in this set of cases?”) rather than confirmatory (“what combinations cause this specific outcome?”), CNA has an advantage. When the causal structure may involve mediation or causal chains, CNA can detect them whereas QCA would require separate analyses for each step. The R package cna implements the method and includes tools for robustness testing.
When to prefer one method over the other depends on the evaluation question and the analyst’s prior knowledge. QCA is the stronger choice when the outcome is known, the conditions are theoretically grounded, and the analyst wants to test specific configurational hypotheses. CNA is the stronger choice when the causal structure is unknown, multiple outcomes may be causally linked, or the analyst wants to minimise the influence of prior specification on the results.






Development evaluation application: CNA for multi-outcome programme pathways



No published development evaluation has used CNA to date, reflecting the method’s recent emergence. A hypothetical application illustrates the method’s potential. A multi-country maternal health programme operates through three intermediate outcomes (trained midwives, improved supply chains, community mobilisation) that interact to produce the final outcome (reduced maternal mortality). A QCA study would require the analyst to specify each intermediate outcome as a separate analysis. CNA could analyse the full causal chain in a single model, identifying which combinations of programme activities lead to trained midwives, which combinations of trained midwives and supply chain improvements lead to community uptake, and which combinations ultimately reduce mortality. The method’s capacity to model multi-outcome causal chains makes it a natural fit for programmes with complex theories of change, provided the analyst exercises care in interpreting the data-driven results against substantive knowledge. Baumgartner & Ambühl (2020) provide the technical foundations for such an application.

title: 5  Configurational Approaches
text:
5.3 The QCA-CNA debate
The relationship between QCA and CNA is not merely a matter of methodological preference; it has become a substantive dispute about what configurational methods can and cannot claim. Thiem (2022) has argued that QCA, as commonly practised, produces fragile results: solutions change substantially with small alterations in calibration thresholds, case selection, or the handling of logical remainders (unobserved configurations). Benchmarking studies using simulated data with known causal structures have shown that QCA’s truth-table approach can recover incorrect solutions when limited diversity is high, when the number of conditions is large relative to the number of cases, or when measurement error is present. These critiques target not the INUS ontology but the specific analytical procedures of truth-table construction and logical minimisation.
Baumgartner & Ambühl (2020) have argued that CNA avoids some of these problems because it does not rely on truth tables and does not require the analyst to make assumptions about unobserved configurations. They contend that CNA’s search algorithm is better suited to recovering causal structures from observational data because it tests all factors symmetrically. Proponents of QCA respond that CNA’s data-driven approach risks overfitting, that the method’s agnosticism about the outcome variable can produce causally implausible models, and that QCA’s requirement for theoretical grounding is a strength rather than a weakness (Schneider & Wagemann, 2012).
The debate remains unresolved. For the evaluator, the practical lesson is threefold. First, neither method should be applied mechanically; both require careful attention to case selection, condition specification, and robustness testing. Second, robustness checks (sensitivity analysis of calibration thresholds for QCA; variation of consistency and coverage thresholds for CNA; permutation tests for both) are not optional but essential. Third, convergence between QCA and CNA results on the same data strengthens confidence in the findings; divergence signals that the results are specification-dependent and should be interpreted cautiously.

The principal strengths of configurational methods are well matched to development evaluation. They handle conjunctural causation: the recognition that outcomes arise from combinations of conditions rather than single causes. They formalise equifinality: different combinations can produce the same outcome, and the methods identify all such pathways rather than estimating a single average effect. The truth tables and solution formulas are transparent and auditable. The methods operate at case numbers (10 to 50) that are common in multi-site and cross-country evaluations.
The limitations are equally important. The limited diversity problem grows rapidly with the number of conditions: five binary conditions produce 32 possible configurations, and a study with 20 cases will observe only a fraction of them. Unobserved configurations require assumptions (simplifying assumptions in QCA, or exclusion in CNA), and these assumptions can drive the results. Calibration sensitivity means that small changes in thresholds can alter the solution, particularly when cases cluster near the crossover point. Configurational methods provide no temporal or mechanistic information: they identify cross-sectional patterns but do not explain how or why a configuration produces the outcome. For mechanistic explanation, evaluators need the generative approaches discussed in the next chapter.






The case against and for configurational methods



Three criticisms recur in the methodological literature. First, QCA is deterministic in a probabilistic world: consistency thresholds below 1.0 are treated as acceptable, but the theoretical framework assumes that sufficient conditions always produce the outcome, creating a tension between the method’s logic and its practice. Second, results are fragile to specification changes: adding or removing a single condition, changing a calibration threshold, or including one additional case can alter the solution substantially. Thiem (2022) document this fragility across published QCA studies. Third, the limited diversity problem means that many solutions rely on assumptions about configurations that were never observed, and different assumptions yield different results.
Proponents respond on each point. On determinism, Schneider & Wagemann (2012) argue that consistency thresholds are an honest acknowledgement that real-world data contain noise, and that the alternative (ignoring deviations) would be worse. On fragility, robustness protocols now exist: analysts can systematically vary calibration thresholds, drop cases, and compare solutions to establish which findings are stable across specifications. On limited diversity, the distinction between complex, intermediate, and parsimonious solutions makes the role of assumptions transparent. The intermediate solution, which incorporates only theoretically plausible simplifying assumptions, offers a principled middle ground. The field has also developed formal robustness tests, including permutation-based significance tests, that were absent from early QCA practice.


Configurational methods identify which combinations of conditions produce the outcome, but they do not explain how those combinations work or why they succeed in some contexts and not others. The next chapter addresses that question through generative and mechanistic approaches: process tracing, contribution analysis, and realist evaluation.
