title: 6  Generative and Mechanistic Approaches
text:
6.1 Theory of change as a precursor
The previous chapter identified which combinations of conditions produce the outcome. It did not explain the mechanism through which those conditions operate, why the same combination succeeds in one site and fails in another, or what the causal chain looks like step by step. The generative logic addresses these questions. It warrants causal claims by identifying how the intervention produces its effects: the “cogs and wheels” (Hedström & Swedberg, 1998) that transmit the causal force from input to outcome. Pawson & Tilley (1997) formalised this as context-mechanism-outcome (CMO) configurations. A mechanism (M) fires only in the right context (C) to produce the outcome (O). The generative logic distinguishes causal explanation (tracing the mechanism) from causal description (establishing that X and Y co-occur or that a configuration is sufficient for Y).
Four method families operationalise this logic, each with different strengths. Process tracing works within a single case at high resolution. Contribution analysis draws on multiple evidence sources to build a programme-level contribution story. Realist evaluation compares CMO configurations across sites. General elimination methodology (GEM) structures the elimination of rival explanations. All four depend on a well-specified theory of change, which is discussed first.
A theory of change (ToC) is not a standalone evaluation method. It is the backbone of all generative approaches. Without a ToC that specifies the causal chain from activities to outcomes, process tracing has nothing to trace, contribution analysis has no contribution story to test, and realist evaluation has no CMO configurations to compare. The distinction that matters for causal inference is between a ToC that supports testing and a logical framework that does not. A testable ToC states each causal link as a proposition that could be wrong: “teacher training improves classroom practice because trained teachers use formative assessment techniques, and formative assessment increases student engagement.” Each clause is independently verifiable. A logical framework lists inputs, outputs, and outcomes in a linear chain without specifying the causal assumptions that connect them.
Quality criteria for ToCs that support causal inference include three features. Each causal link should be stated as a testable assumption. Alternative causal pathways should be acknowledged alongside the primary pathway. Contextual conditions under which the mechanism is expected to fire should be specified. A ToC that meets these criteria provides the roadmap for process tracing (which links to test), contribution analysis (which links to gather evidence on), and realist evaluation (which CMO configurations to compare).

title: 6  Generative and Mechanistic Approaches
text:
6.2 Process tracing
Process tracing examines the causal chain from intervention to outcome within a single case (Beach & Pedersen, 2019). The method operates at high resolution within one case rather than comparing across cases. The evaluator identifies the hypothesised mechanism (from the ToC), specifies the observable implications that the mechanism would leave if it were active, and then searches for those traces in the empirical record. If the predicted traces are found, confidence in the causal claim increases. If they are absent, confidence decreases.
Beach & Pedersen (2019) distinguish three types of process tracing, each suited to a different evaluation question. Theory-testing process tracing starts with a fully specified mechanism and tests whether it operated in the case under study. The evaluator derives predictions from the theory and checks them against evidence. Theory-building process tracing works in the opposite direction: the evaluator examines the empirical record to induce a plausible mechanism that explains the observed outcome. Explaining-outcome process tracing combines elements of both, tracing back from the outcome to construct a case-specific explanation that may draw on multiple mechanisms. Theory-testing is the most common in evaluation because evaluators usually have a ToC that specifies the expected mechanism.
The evidence pieces that process tracing examines are causal-process observations (CPOs). Each CPO carries diagnostic value: it updates the probability that the hypothesised mechanism was active. A programme document showing that the implementing agency followed the predicted decision sequence is a CPO. An interview in which a key actor describes behavioural changes consistent with the hypothesised mechanism is a CPO. The formal treatment of how CPOs update causal confidence belongs to the diagnostic logic and is covered in Chapter 7. Many practitioners combine generative and diagnostic logics in practice, using process tracing to identify the mechanism and Bayesian updating to quantify the evidential weight of each observation.


Befani & Stedman-Bryce (2017) demonstrate this combination in practice, applying Bayesian updating to process-tracing evidence in an impact evaluation, showing how each piece of evidence formally shifts the probability that the hypothesised mechanism was active.
Process tracing requires intensive within-case data: programme documents, administrative records, meeting minutes, interviews with implementers and beneficiaries, direct observation, and secondary literature. The method works at n=1, but the depth of engagement with the single case is high. In development evaluation, process tracing is well suited to tracing how a policy reform produced or failed to produce the intended change in a single country, sector, or programme site. It is common in evaluations of complex governance interventions, institutional reforms, and policy processes where the causal chain is long and context-dependent.


Piper et al. (2018) trace how Kenya’s Tusome national literacy programme scaled up successfully, identifying the specific mechanisms (coaching, materials, accountability) through which the intervention produced learning gains — a detailed process tracing of a single-country education reform.

title: 6  Generative and Mechanistic Approaches
text:
6.3 Contribution analysis
Contribution analysis builds and iteratively strengthens a “contribution story” that explains how the intervention contributed to the observed outcome (Mayne, 2001). The method does not claim to prove attribution; it claims to establish a credible account of contribution, supported by evidence at each step of the causal chain.
Mayne’s framework proceeds in six steps. First, set out the attribution problem: what causal question needs answering, and what is the intervention’s claimed role? Second, develop or refine the theory of change, identifying the key causal links and the assumptions behind each. Third, gather existing evidence on the ToC: what is already known about whether each link holds? Fourth, assemble and assess the contribution story: given the available evidence, how strong is the claim that the intervention contributed to the outcome? Fifth, seek additional evidence where the story is weak: which links lack supporting evidence, and what new data would strengthen or weaken the claim? Sixth, revise and strengthen the contribution story in light of the new evidence. The process is iterative: steps five and six may cycle several times.
Contribution analysis differs from process tracing in scope and evidence strategy. Process tracing traces a single causal chain within one case at high resolution, relying on CPOs. Contribution analysis draws on multiple evidence sources (quantitative data, stakeholder interviews, programme records, secondary literature, monitoring data) to assess the plausibility of the entire contribution story. It is designed for programme-level evaluation rather than single-case research. The contribution story is the ToC, tested against evidence. Each causal link in the ToC is assessed for strength, and the overall claim is only as strong as the weakest link.
The distinction between strong and weak links deserves emphasis. Mayne (2012) proposes a four-level scale for assessing each causal link: not addressed (no evidence gathered), not supported (evidence does not confirm the link), plausible (some evidence supports the link but alternatives remain), and confirmed (strong evidence supports the link and alternatives have been addressed). A contribution story in which every link reaches at least the “plausible” level represents a credible contribution claim. A story with one or more links at “not supported” identifies exactly where the causal chain breaks down. This graduated assessment is what separates contribution analysis from a narrative description of the programme: each link is independently evaluated, and the overall claim is transparent about its evidential basis.






Development evaluation application: contribution analysis of a health systems programme



Dossou et al. (2020) applied contribution analysis to evaluate a performance-based financing programme in Benin. The evaluation team mapped the theory of change from financial incentives through health facility management improvements to increased service utilisation. Each causal link was assessed using facility-level data, health worker interviews, and administrative records. The analysis identified that the link between incentives and management improvements was well supported, but the link between management improvements and patient utilisation was weak in facilities with low baseline demand. Contribution analysis revealed that the programme contributed to service delivery improvements in facilities with existing patient demand but had limited impact where demand-side barriers (distance, cost to patients, cultural norms) were binding. The graduated assessment allowed the evaluation to produce a differentiated conclusion rather than a binary “worked/did not work” judgement.


When to prefer contribution analysis over process tracing depends on the evaluation question and the available evidence. Contribution analysis is the stronger choice when the evaluation covers a programme rather than a single case, when multiple evidence types are available, and when the commissioner needs a narrative that explains contribution across the full causal chain. Process tracing is the stronger choice when the question requires high-resolution tracing of a specific mechanism within one case, and when detailed within-case evidence (documents, testimony, records) is available.

title: 6  Generative and Mechanistic Approaches
text:
6.4 Realist evaluation
Realist evaluation asks “what works, for whom, in what circumstances, and why?” (Pawson & Tilley, 1997). This question structures the entire approach. The unit of analysis is the CMO configuration: a specified context (C) triggers a specified mechanism (M) to produce a specified outcome (O). The programme does not “cause” the outcome directly; it provides resources and opportunities that activate mechanisms in specific contexts.
CMO configurations are best explained through example. A community health worker programme (the intervention) may improve maternal health outcomes (O) in rural areas with weak formal health infrastructure (C) because the programme activates trust between health workers and mothers, leading to sustained uptake of antenatal care (M). In urban areas with strong hospital access (a different C), the same programme may activate a different mechanism (supplementing rather than substituting for formal care) and produce a different outcome (small marginal improvement rather than transformative change). Realist evaluation identifies and compares these configurations rather than estimating a single average effect.


Greenhalgh et al. (2009) provide a worked example of realist evaluation applied to a health service modernisation programme in London, tracing how different contextual conditions activated different mechanisms to produce divergent outcomes across sites.
The comparison logic in realist evaluation differs from statistical comparison. The goal is not to establish whether the programme “works on average” but to identify which contexts activate which mechanisms to produce which outcomes. Case selection is purposive: the evaluator selects cases that vary on the contextual conditions hypothesised to matter, and collects data on the mechanisms hypothesised to operate. The analysis compares CMO configurations across sites, refining the programme theory with each case.
Realist synthesis extends this logic to evidence reviews. Rather than pooling effect sizes from multiple studies, realist synthesis extracts CMO configurations from each study and compares them across the evidence base. The output is a refined programme theory that specifies the conditions under which the intervention works, the mechanisms through which it works, and the outcomes it produces. Realist synthesis is well suited to reviews of small-n evaluations because it does not require standardised effect sizes or statistical homogeneity.
In development evaluation, realist evaluation is well suited to complex programmes implemented across diverse contexts: multi-country governance initiatives, community-driven development programmes, and health system strengthening efforts where the same design produces different results in different settings.

title: 6  Generative and Mechanistic Approaches
text:
6.5 General elimination methodology
General elimination methodology (GEM) warrants causal claims by systematically eliminating rival explanations (Scriven, 2008). The logic is Popperian: if all plausible alternative explanations have been tested and ruled out, the remaining explanation is the most credible. GEM does not prove causation; it eliminates alternatives until the causal claim survives.
The practical steps require disciplined application. The evaluator begins by listing all plausible rival explanations for the observed outcome. These include standard validity threats (maturation, history, selection, regression to the mean) as well as context-specific alternatives (co-occurring interventions, changes in political leadership, external shocks, Hawthorne effects). Shadish et al. (2002) provide the canonical taxonomy of validity threats that GEM draws upon, though the list should always be extended with programme-specific rivals derived from contextual knowledge. For each rival, the evaluator specifies what observable evidence would support it and what evidence would contradict it. Evidence is then gathered to test each rival. Rivals that the evidence contradicts are eliminated. Rivals that the evidence cannot disconfirm remain as standing alternatives that qualify the causal conclusion.
The strength of the remaining causal claim depends on three factors: how many rivals were considered, how rigorously each was tested, and how plausible the surviving alternatives are. An evaluation that considers only two or three obvious rivals and fails to test context-specific alternatives provides a weaker warrant than one that conducts a systematic search. GEM is often used alongside process tracing or contribution analysis to structure the elimination of rival hypotheses rather than as a standalone design. Its value is as an inferential discipline: it forces the evaluator to consider and test alternatives rather than building a case for the preferred explanation alone. Befani (2012) note that GEM complements Bayesian process tracing: the rivals that GEM identifies become the alternative hypotheses against which the diagnostic tests in Chapter 7 are calibrated.






Development evaluation application: GEM in a governance reform evaluation



Palenberg (2011) applied a structured rival-elimination approach in an evaluation of governance support programmes in sub-Saharan Africa. The evaluation team identified seven plausible rival explanations for observed improvements in public financial management (including donor coordination effects, leadership transitions, and parallel reform programmes funded by other agencies). Each rival was tested against documentary evidence, key informant interviews, and administrative data. Four rivals were eliminated; three survived as partial alternative explanations. The final causal claim was that the programme contributed to the observed improvements in conjunction with favourable leadership conditions, a more qualified and credible conclusion than an unstructured assessment would have produced.

The principal strengths of generative methods are well matched to the questions that development evaluators face most often. They provide causal explanation: not just whether the intervention worked, but how and why it worked. They work at n=1 (process tracing) or small n (realist evaluation). They are closely aligned with the theory-based evaluation tradition that most development evaluators already practise. They address “how” and “why” questions that counterfactual and configurational methods cannot answer.
The limitations are important. Generative methods are labour-intensive. Process tracing requires deep engagement with a single case: weeks of document review, multiple rounds of interviews, and iterative analysis. Contribution analysis requires multiple evidence sources and several iterative cycles. Realist evaluation requires data collection on contexts and mechanisms across multiple sites, not just on outcomes. Generalisability depends on replication: a process tracing study of one country cannot, on its own, tell the evaluator whether the mechanism operates in other countries. Mechanism identification carries a risk of subjectivity: two analysts examining the same evidence may construct different mechanistic accounts, and the method provides no formal procedure for resolving the disagreement.






The case against and for generative methods



Three criticisms recur in the methodological literature. First, mechanism claims are difficult to falsify because the mechanism is not directly observed; the evaluator infers its presence from observable traces, and the inference depends on the evaluator’s theoretical framework. Second, theory-based approaches risk confirmation bias: the ToC specifies what evidence to seek, and the evaluator may attend selectively to evidence that confirms the hypothesised mechanism while neglecting disconfirming evidence. Third, contribution analysis and realist evaluation can become tautological if the ToC is not independently testable: the programme theory explains the outcome, and the outcome validates the programme theory.
Proponents respond on each point. On falsifiability, process tracing generates specific predictions that can fail. A theory-testing process trace that predicts five observable implications and finds only two has produced a negative result; the method is falsifiable at the level of individual tests. On confirmation bias, the iterative design of contribution analysis explicitly requires the evaluator to seek additional evidence where the story is weak, and GEM’s rival-elimination logic forces attention to alternative explanations. On tautology, realist evaluation compares CMO configurations across sites: if the hypothesised mechanism fires only in some contexts and not others, the cross-site comparison provides a test that a single-site study cannot. The diagnostic logic covered in the next chapter adds a further layer of rigour by quantifying how much each piece of evidence should shift confidence in the causal claim.


The generative logic identifies the mechanism, but it does not formally quantify how much each piece of evidence should change confidence in the causal claim. The next chapter provides that formal machinery through the diagnostic logic and Bayesian updating.

title: 7  Diagnostic Approaches
text:
7.1 The diagnostic logic
The previous chapter showed how generative methods identify the mechanism connecting intervention to outcome. Process tracing, in particular, collects causal-process observations and uses them to update confidence in the hypothesised causal chain. But the generative logic does not specify how much each piece of evidence should change that confidence. Two evaluators examining the same CPO may reach different conclusions about its evidential weight. The diagnostic logic provides the formal machinery for resolving this problem. It treats each piece of evidence as a diagnostic test and uses Bayesian updating to quantify how much the evidence shifts the probability that the causal claim is true.
The diagnostic logic is not a rival to the generative logic; it is its inferential engine. Process tracing identifies what to look for. Bayesian updating determines how much what is found should matter. The combination of the two produces Bayesian process tracing, the method that occupies most of this chapter. The chapter also covers the practical challenges of estimating the probabilities that Bayesian updating requires, and a recent innovation that uses computational simulation to generate those estimates when empirical frequencies and subjective elicitation are both infeasible.
The diagnostic logic evaluates causal claims through the probative value of evidence. Each piece of evidence is treated as a diagnostic test with two properties: sensitivity (the probability of observing the evidence if the causal claim is true) and the false-positive rate (the probability of observing the evidence if the causal claim is false). These two properties determine the likelihood ratio, which measures the evidential weight of the observation.
The Bayesian machinery proceeds in three steps. The evaluator begins with prior odds that the causal claim is true, based on available background knowledge. The evaluator then observes a piece of evidence and computes its likelihood ratio: sensitivity divided by the false-positive rate. The prior odds are multiplied by the likelihood ratio to produce posterior odds. If a second piece of evidence is observed, the posterior odds from the first update become the prior odds for the second, and the process repeats. The result is a transparent, sequential updating of causal confidence as evidence accumulates.
The updating rule can be stated formally. Let \(H\) denote the hypothesis that the intervention caused the outcome, and let \(\neg H\) denote the alternative (the outcome would have occurred without the intervention). The prior odds are \(O(H) = P(H) / P(\neg H)\). For a piece of evidence \(E\), the likelihood ratio is:
\[
LR = \frac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)}
\tag{7.1}\]
where \(P(E \mid H)\) is the probability of observing the evidence if the hypothesis is true (sensitivity) and \(P(E \mid \neg H)\) is the probability of observing the evidence if the hypothesis is false (the false-positive rate). The posterior odds after observing \(E\) are:
\[
O(H \mid E) = O(H) \times LR
\tag{7.2}\]
When multiple independent pieces of evidence \(E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k\) are observed, the posterior odds are the product of the prior odds and all likelihood ratios: \(O(H \mid E_1, \ldots, E_k) = O(H) \times LR_1 \times LR_2 \times \cdots \times LR_k\). The posterior probability can be recovered from the posterior odds as \(P(H \mid E) = O(H \mid E) / (1 + O(H \mid E))\).
The relationship to process tracing is direct. The causal-process observations that process tracing collects are the diagnostic tests. A programme document confirming that the implementing agency followed the predicted decision sequence is a test with known sensitivity and false-positive rate. An interview in which a key actor describes behavioural changes consistent with the hypothesis is another test. The diagnostic logic provides the formal framework for combining these tests into an overall assessment of the causal claim.

title: 7  Diagnostic Approaches
text:
7.2 Bayesian process tracing in practice
The political science literature formalised four classic process-tracing tests, each defined by its combination of sensitivity and specificity (Collier, 2011). The straw-in-the-wind test has low sensitivity and low specificity: the evidence is consistent with the hypothesis but also consistent with alternatives. Passing the test slightly increases confidence; failing it slightly decreases confidence. The hoop test has high sensitivity but low specificity: the evidence is expected if the hypothesis is true (high sensitivity), but it is also expected under many alternatives (low specificity). Failing a hoop test strongly undermines the hypothesis; passing it is only mildly confirmatory. The smoking-gun test has low sensitivity but high specificity: the evidence is unlikely to be observed (low sensitivity) but, if observed, is strongly confirmatory because it is very unlikely under alternatives (high specificity). The doubly decisive test has both high sensitivity and high specificity: the evidence is expected if the hypothesis is true and unexpected if it is false. A single doubly decisive test can confirm the hypothesis on its own.
Table 7.1 maps these four tests to their sensitivity and specificity profiles.

In practice, the evaluator maps each CPO to one of these test types, assigns approximate sensitivity and false-positive rate values, and computes the likelihood ratio. The updating is iterative: evidence accumulates across multiple tests, and the posterior odds after the final test represent the overall confidence in the causal claim. The sequence in which evidence is considered does not affect the final posterior odds, provided the tests are independent. Independence is a strong assumption, and its violations are a practical challenge discussed below.
A worked example illustrates the mechanics. Suppose an evaluator assesses whether a parliamentary strengthening programme caused improved legislative oversight in a single country. The prior probability is set at 0.5 (agnostic), giving prior odds of 1:1. Three pieces of evidence are collected.
First, a hoop test: parliamentary records show that oversight committees met regularly after the programme began. This evidence is expected if the hypothesis is true (sensitivity = 0.90) but also expected under alternatives such as a concurrent governance reform (false-positive rate = 0.60). The likelihood ratio is \(0.90/0.60 = 1.5\). Posterior odds after the first test: \(1.0 \times 1.5 = 1.5\), corresponding to a posterior probability of 0.60.
Second, a smoking-gun test: internal government documents show that committee members explicitly referenced programme training materials when designing new oversight procedures. This evidence is less likely to be observed (sensitivity = 0.40) but very unlikely under the alternative (false-positive rate = 0.05). The likelihood ratio is \(0.40/0.05 = 8.0\). Posterior odds after the second test: \(1.5 \times 8.0 = 12.0\), corresponding to a posterior probability of 0.92.
Third, a straw-in-the-wind test: government officials express positive views about the programme in interviews. This evidence is somewhat expected under the hypothesis (sensitivity = 0.70) and also somewhat expected under alternatives, given social desirability (false-positive rate = 0.50). The likelihood ratio is \(0.70/0.50 = 1.4\). Posterior odds after the third test: \(12.0 \times 1.4 = 16.8\), corresponding to a posterior probability of 0.94.
The final posterior probability of 0.94 reflects moderate-to-strong confidence in the causal claim. The updating trail is fully transparent: a reader who disagrees with any probability estimate can substitute an alternative value and recompute. The smoking-gun evidence (the internal documents) contributed the most inferential weight, as one would expect from its high likelihood ratio.






Development evaluation application: Bayesian process tracing of policy influence



Befani & Stedman-Bryce (2017) applied Bayesian process tracing to evaluate the impact of a research programme on policy change in the health sector. The evaluation team identified observable implications of the hypothesised influence pathway (uptake of research findings in policy documents, citation in parliamentary debates, changes in budget allocations consistent with recommendations). Each piece of evidence was classified as a hoop, smoking-gun, or straw-in-the-wind test, and sensitivity and false-positive rate values were elicited from the evaluation team using structured protocols. The sequential updating produced a posterior probability that the research programme had contributed to the observed policy change, with the strongest evidential weight coming from budget allocation changes that mirrored the programme’s specific recommendations. The worked example demonstrated that Bayesian process tracing can produce formal, auditable causal conclusions for single-case policy evaluations.

title: 7  Diagnostic Approaches
text:
7.3 Practical challenges and solutions
Befani (2020) identifies three practical challenges that arise when applying Bayesian updating to theory-based evaluation. The first is probability estimation. The Bayesian machinery requires numerical values for sensitivity and the false-positive rate, but evaluators rarely have empirical frequency data for the causal mechanisms under study. How often does a programme document confirming the predicted decision sequence appear when the hypothesis is true? How often does it appear when the hypothesis is false? These frequencies are seldom known. Befani (2020) proposes two solutions: using qualitative confidence descriptors (“very likely,” “unlikely”) anchored to numerical ranges, and using structured elicitation protocols in which the evaluation team collectively estimates the probabilities.
The second challenge is confirmation bias and conservatism. Evaluation teams that have invested in a theory of change may assign overly high sensitivity values to confirming evidence (they expect to find it because they believe the hypothesis) and overly low false-positive rates (they underestimate the chance of finding it under alternatives). Befani (2020) recommends explicit “red team” exercises in which a team member argues the rival hypothesis and re-estimates the probabilities from that perspective. Conservatism, the opposite problem, arises when evaluators are reluctant to assign extreme probability values, compressing likelihood ratios toward 1 and producing updates that are too small. The solution is to anchor probability estimates in concrete counterfactual reasoning: “if the programme had not been implemented, how likely is it that this document would exist?”
The third challenge is evidence packages. Bayesian updating assumes that each test is independent: the result of one test does not predict the result of another. In practice, evidence often comes in bundles. Interview testimony from three programme staff members may all reflect the same institutional narrative rather than providing three independent observations. Befani (2020) proposes setting boundaries on independent observations by asking whether each piece of evidence could, in principle, turn out differently from the others. Evidence that cannot vary independently should be treated as a single observation with a single likelihood ratio, not as multiple independent tests.

title: 7  Diagnostic Approaches
text:
7.4 Simulated probabilities for diagnostic evaluation
When empirical frequency data are unavailable and subjective elicitation is difficult, Befani et al. (2021) propose a third approach: using computational simulation to generate the probability estimates that Bayesian updating requires. The method works as follows. The evaluation team builds an agent-based model that encodes the hypothesised causal mechanism. The model is run under two conditions: one in which the mechanism is active (the hypothesis is true) and one in which it is not (the hypothesis is false). For each condition, the model generates the expected pattern of observable evidence. The proportion of simulation runs that produce the observed evidence pattern under the “true” condition estimates sensitivity. The proportion that produce it under the “false” condition estimates the false-positive rate.
Befani et al. (2021) demonstrate the approach with a health policy application, showing that simulation-derived probability estimates produce posterior odds that are consistent with expert judgement but are more transparent and reproducible. The approach is warranted when three conditions hold: the causal mechanism is well enough understood to build a plausible simulation model, empirical frequency data do not exist, and the evaluation team lacks the domain expertise for confident subjective elicitation. Computational simulation as a broader tool for small-n evaluation is discussed in Chapter 9.

title: 7  Diagnostic Approaches
text:
7.5 Strengths, limitations, and applicability
Table 7.2 summarises the diagnostic methods covered in this chapter.



Table 7.2: Diagnostic methods and their applicability












Method
Input
Probability source
Output
Main limitation




Bayesian process tracing
Causal-process observations from within-case analysis

Probability estimation remains subjective; independence assumption often violated


Simulated diagnostic evaluation
Agent-based model of the mechanism
Simulation-derived frequencies

The principal strengths of the diagnostic logic are formality and transparency. The updating process is explicit: prior odds, likelihood ratios, and posterior odds are all stated and can be inspected, challenged, and revised. The framework is cumulative: new evidence can be incorporated as it becomes available, and the sequence of updates creates a transparent audit trail. The diagnostic logic is the only logic in this book that formally quantifies uncertainty about the causal claim.
The limitations centre on the probability estimation bottleneck. Sensitivity and false-positive rate values are rarely available from empirical data. Subjective elicitation can produce values that reflect the evaluator’s priors rather than genuine evidential weight. The independence assumption is frequently violated in practice, and violations inflate the apparent strength of the evidence. The learning curve is steep: evaluators trained in qualitative or theory-based traditions may find the Bayesian formalism unfamiliar. Befani (2020) notes that these are implementation challenges, not fundamental objections: the diagnostic logic is sound; the difficulty lies in applying it well.






The case against and for diagnostic evaluation



Two criticisms recur. First, the probabilities are “made up”: in the absence of empirical frequency data, sensitivity and false-positive rates are subjective estimates, and the Bayesian formalism lends a false precision to what is ultimately a qualitative judgement. Second, the method is too complex for routine evaluation practice: the Bayesian machinery requires training that most evaluation teams do not have, and the probability estimation step adds substantial time to the evaluation.
Proponents respond that all causal inference involves implicit probability assessment. When a process-tracing analyst concludes that a smoking-gun piece of evidence “strongly confirms” the hypothesis, the analyst is making an implicit claim about its likelihood ratio. The Bayesian framework makes that claim explicit and challengeable, which is an improvement over implicit reasoning even when the numerical estimates are imprecise. On complexity, Befani (2020) demonstrates that structured elicitation with qualitative descriptors (“very likely,” “unlikely”) reduces the technical burden without abandoning the formal structure. The simulated probability approach of Befani et al. (2021) further reduces reliance on subjective judgement. The diagnostic logic is demanding, but its demands are the price of making evidential reasoning transparent.


The next chapter turns from individual-case analysis to approaches that draw on the knowledge and experience of programme participants and stakeholders: participatory and agency-based methods for causal inference.

title: 8  Participatory and Agency-Based Approaches
text:
8.1 Most Significant Change
The methods covered so far treat the evaluator as the epistemic authority. The evaluator designs the study, specifies the causal hypothesis, collects the data, and warrants the causal claim. The participatory logic shifts part of that authority to programme participants and stakeholders. Beneficiaries have privileged access to information that no external evaluator can observe directly: whether their behaviour changed because of the programme, what alternative explanations they consider plausible, and what unexpected changes occurred. The participatory logic uses this access as a source of causal evidence, not merely as a perspective to be documented.
An important distinction must be stated at the outset. Participatory methods do not establish cause and effect in the same sense as counterfactual, configurational, or diagnostic designs. They produce stakeholder-validated contribution claims: participants report what changed, attribute the change to the programme, and independent verification assesses whether the attribution is plausible. The causal warrant is “those who experienced the change attribute it to the intervention, and independent reviewers find the claim credible.” That warrant is weaker on confounding than a randomised design or a Bayesian process trace, because no formal mechanism rules out alternative explanations. The strength of participatory methods lies elsewhere: they capture causal knowledge that external observation cannot access, and they surface unexpected outcomes that pre-specified indicators would miss. They are strongest when combined with other logics, a point this chapter returns to at the end.
A second distinction matters. “Participatory methods” is a broad term covering everything from participatory rural appraisal to focus group discussions. This chapter is not about participatory methods in general. It covers two specific methods that use stakeholder knowledge to support contribution claims: Most Significant Change (MSC) and outcome harvesting. Both include structured verification procedures that go beyond recording what participants say.
Most Significant Change is a story-based method that collects change narratives from programme participants and stakeholders, subjects them to structured selection, and verifies the selected narratives against independent evidence (Davies & Dart, 2005). The method proceeds in three steps.
The first step is story collection. Programme participants and field staff are asked an open-ended question: “what was the most significant change that occurred as a result of the programme?” The question is deliberately open. It does not specify which outcome domain to report on, and it invites respondents to describe changes that the evaluator may not have anticipated. Each story includes a description of the change and the respondent’s explanation of why the programme contributed to it.
The second step is systematic selection. Panels at multiple levels of the programme (field offices, regional offices, headquarters) review the collected stories and select those they judge most significant. Each panel records its selection rationale: why this story, and not others, represents the most important change. The selection process surfaces the values and priorities of different stakeholder groups, which is itself evaluative information.
The third step is verification. Selected stories are cross-checked against programme records, third-party observation, or follow-up interviews. Verification transforms the story from testimony into evidence. An unverified story is a stakeholder perception; a verified story is a contribution claim supported by corroboration. The strength of the causal warrant depends on the rigour of this step. Effective verification involves at least three components: triangulation against documentary sources (programme records, administrative data, media reports), follow-up interviews with people named in or affected by the reported change, and assessment by reviewers who were not involved in the original story collection. Davies & Dart (2005) emphasise that verification is not a binary judgement (true or false) but a graduated assessment of plausibility, specificity, and corroboration strength.
MSC does not estimate an effect size. It does not claim to rule out confounding. What it produces is a curated set of verified change narratives in which the programme’s contribution has been assessed by those closest to the change and corroborated by independent evidence. The method captures unexpected and emergent outcomes that pre-specified indicators would miss. It works at very small n, including a single programme site. It surfaces the outcomes that matter most to participants rather than to the evaluator or the commissioner.
The limitations are equally clear. Not all participants submit stories, and those who do may be systematically different from those who do not: more engaged, more literate, or more favourably disposed toward the programme. The selection panels’ criteria may reflect organisational priorities rather than evidential weight. Aggregation across stories is difficult because each story is unique and context-specific. The method does not address confounding: if a concurrent policy change also affected the participants, MSC provides no formal way to separate the programme’s contribution from the policy’s contribution. In development evaluation, MSC is widely used in NGO and civil-society evaluations of complex programmes with diffuse or hard-to-predict outcomes, including community development, empowerment, and governance interventions.

title: 8  Participatory and Agency-Based Approaches
text:
Development evaluation application: MSC in a community development programme



Willetts & Crawford (2007) applied MSC to evaluate a community water and sanitation programme in Vanuatu. Story collection yielded narratives from community members describing changes ranging from improved health outcomes to shifts in gender dynamics around water-point management. Selection panels at local, provincial, and national levels identified the most significant changes and recorded their selection rationale. Verification included follow-up visits to story locations, cross-checking with health clinic records, and interviews with community leaders not involved in the programme. The MSC process revealed that the most significant change from the community’s perspective was not the health improvement (which the programme had targeted) but the empowerment of women who gained management roles at water points, an outcome that pre-specified indicators had not anticipated.

title: 8  Participatory and Agency-Based Approaches
text:
8.2 Outcome harvesting
Outcome harvesting reverses the direction of evaluation inquiry (Wilson-Grau & Britt, 2012). Rather than starting from a theory of change and testing whether predicted outcomes occurred, outcome harvesting starts from observed outcomes and traces them back to the intervention’s contribution. The direction is harvest first, attribute second.
The six-step procedure proceeds as follows. First, design the harvest: define the scope, intended users, and available resources. Second, gather data and draft outcome descriptions: programme staff and partners identify changes they have observed and describe the intervention’s claimed contribution to each. Third, engage change agents: those who were directly involved review and refine the outcome descriptions, correcting errors and adding detail. Fourth, substantiate: independent reviewers verify each outcome against documentary and third-party evidence. Fifth, analyse and interpret: look for patterns across the harvested outcomes. Sixth, support use of findings: present results to intended users.
Substantiation is the critical step for causal credibility. Each harvested outcome is reviewed by someone independent of the programme, who assesses whether the evidence supports the claimed contribution. Substantiation typically involves three procedures. First, the reviewer checks whether the outcome actually occurred, using documentary evidence, third-party testimony, or direct observation. Second, the reviewer assesses whether the programme’s claimed contribution is plausible: did the programme activities precede the outcome, and is there a plausible causal pathway connecting them? Third, the reviewer considers alternative explanations: could the outcome have occurred without the programme’s contribution, and if so, how likely is that scenario? Wilson-Grau & Britt (2012) describe substantiation as the step that separates outcome harvesting from unstructured testimony collection. Without substantiation, outcome harvesting produces unverified contribution claims. With substantiation, it produces independently reviewed contribution evidence. The difference is significant, and evaluators who use outcome harvesting without completing the substantiation step weaken the method’s warrant considerably.






Development evaluation application: outcome harvesting for a policy advocacy programme



Wilson-Grau (2015) applied outcome harvesting to evaluate the influence of an international advocacy network on land governance policy in several Central American countries. Programme staff and partners identified 62 outcomes (changes in policies, practices, and relationships) that they attributed to the network’s advocacy activities. Independent substantiators reviewed each outcome against documentary evidence (legislative records, media reports, official correspondence) and third-party interviews. Thirty-eight outcomes were substantiated with strong evidence, sixteen were partially supported, and eight lacked sufficient corroboration. The analysis identified patterns across the substantiated outcomes: the network’s advocacy was most effective when it combined direct engagement with policymakers and evidence provision, and least effective when it relied on media campaigns alone. The graduated substantiation allowed the evaluation to produce differentiated conclusions about which advocacy strategies worked and which did not.


Outcome harvesting and contribution analysis both build contribution stories, but from opposite directions. Contribution analysis starts from the theory of change and gathers evidence to test each causal link. Outcome harvesting starts from what happened and works backward to identify which intervention activities contributed. The two methods can be combined: outcome harvesting identifies the changes, and contribution analysis tests the causal logic connecting intervention to change. In development evaluation, outcome harvesting is well suited to advocacy and policy-influence programmes where outcomes are diffuse and non-linear, to multi-stakeholder initiatives, and to complex systems interventions where the theory of change is emergent rather than pre-specified.

text:
8.3 Strengths, limitations, and applicability
Table 8.1 summarises the two methods.

Method
n range

Outcome harvesting

The principal strengths of participatory methods are their capacity to capture complexity, emergence, and unexpected outcomes. They centre participant voice and insider knowledge. They work at small n without requiring repeated measurement or pre-specified outcome indicators. They are less reliant on the evaluator’s prior theory about what the programme should produce.
The limitations are substantial and should not be minimised. Both methods rely on participant honesty, recall, and willingness to report. Social desirability bias can lead participants to over-attribute change to the programme. Neither method provides a formal mechanism for ruling out confounding or alternative explanations. MSC’s selection process reflects the values and power dynamics of the selecting panels. Outcome harvesting’s post-hoc attribution is vulnerable to cherry-picking if the substantiation step is weak. These limitations mean that participatory methods are rarely sufficient on their own for causal inference. They are strongest when combined with other logics: a process trace can test the mechanisms behind a harvested outcome, a diagnostic evaluation can quantify the evidential weight of a verified MSC story, and a configurational analysis can identify which contextual conditions are associated with the changes that participants report. The integration chapter (Chapter 11) discusses these combinations in detail.






The case against and for participatory causal methods



Two criticisms recur. First, stakeholder testimony is subjective and prone to social desirability bias, recall error, and strategic misreporting. Participants may attribute change to the programme because they know that is what the evaluator wants to hear, or because they want the programme to continue. Second, post-hoc attribution (identifying outcomes first and attributing them to the programme second) inverts the logic of hypothesis testing. The risk of cherry-picking favourable outcomes is real if the substantiation step is weak or absent.
Proponents respond on both points. On subjectivity, all causal inference involves judgement; participatory methods make the source of judgement explicit. When a process-tracing analyst interprets a document as evidence for a mechanism, the analyst is exercising subjective judgement too. The difference is that participatory methods locate the judgement with those who experienced the change, which gives access to information that external analysts cannot obtain. Verification and substantiation steps address (though do not eliminate) the risk of bias. On post-hoc attribution, outcome harvesting’s six-step procedure includes independent substantiation precisely to guard against cherry-picking. The method’s designers explicitly warn that omitting substantiation undermines the warrant. The participatory logic produces contribution evidence, not proof of cause and effect. Its value is as one source of causal evidence within a multi-logic evaluation design.


The methods covered in Part II so far rely on empirical evidence drawn from cases, stakeholders, or within-case analysis. The next chapter introduces a different kind of tool: computational simulation, which supports small-n evaluation not by collecting new evidence but by generating synthetic scenarios, estimating probabilities, and exploring intervention dynamics in silico.

title: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs
text:
9.1 Agent-based modelling
Agent-based modelling and system dynamics are not themselves small-n evaluation methods. They are modelling frameworks that operate at any scale, from a handful of agents to millions. This chapter positions them as complementary tools that strengthen small-n evaluations by filling three specific gaps: generating counterfactual scenarios when empirical comparators are unavailable, estimating probabilities needed for diagnostic evaluation, and exploring the emergent dynamics of complex interventions. The chapter concentrates on how simulation supports causal inference in small-n contexts, rather than on computational technique. Readers seeking programming guidance should consult Gilbert & Troitzsch (2005) for agent-based modelling and Sterman (2000) for system dynamics.
An agent-based model (ABM) represents a system as a collection of autonomous agents that follow behavioural rules. Each agent perceives its local environment, interacts with neighbouring agents, and updates its state accordingly. The aggregate behaviour of the system emerges from these micro-level interactions rather than from a set of top-down equations. This bottom-up architecture makes ABM well suited to modelling causal mechanisms, because the analyst must specify the rules that produce the outcome rather than merely estimating a relationship between variables.
Three features of ABM are relevant for small-n evaluation. First, ABM can generate the probability estimates that Bayesian process tracing requires. Befani et al. (2021) demonstrated this approach by building a simulation of the causal mechanism under evaluation, running it thousands of times with different parameter values, and using the resulting distributions as prior and likelihood estimates. This “third way” sits alongside empirical frequency data and subjective expert elicitation as a source of probability estimates (Chapter 7). When empirical frequency data are unavailable and expert elicitation is contested, simulation offers a transparent and replicable alternative. Second, ABM can model the mechanism chains that process tracing investigates. A theory of change posits that a training programme improves teacher practice, which improves student outcomes. An ABM can represent teachers as agents with varying skill levels, model the training intervention as a rule change, and observe whether the simulated mechanism chain produces the predicted pattern. If the ABM reproduces the observed outcome only under specific parametric assumptions, that constrains the set of plausible causal accounts. Third, ABM can construct virtual counterfactuals for cases where no empirical comparison group exists. A one-off policy reform in a single country cannot be compared with a control country, but an ABM calibrated to the pre-reform period can simulate the trajectory that would have occurred without the reform.
Validation is the central challenge. An ABM is only as credible as its rules, and the analyst must justify those rules with empirical evidence or well-established theory. Pattern-oriented modelling offers one approach: the analyst specifies multiple empirical patterns that the model must reproduce simultaneously, which constrains the parameter space and reduces the risk of overfitting to a single outcome. Even so, simulation results should be treated as structured thought experiments rather than as empirical findings. They sharpen causal reasoning; they do not replace empirical evidence.

title: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs
text: Maroulis et al. (2010) use an ABM of school choice in Chicago to show how agent interactions produce emergent enrolment patterns that a regression model would miss — a published example of ABM applied to education policy evaluation. The ABM tradition traces to Epstein & Axtell (1996), whose Growing Artificial Societies demonstrated how complex social outcomes emerge from simple agent rules.
crumbs: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs

title: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs
text:
9.2 System dynamics
System dynamics (SD) models represent a system as a set of stocks (accumulations), flows (rates of change), and feedback loops. A causal loop diagram maps the qualitative structure: which variables reinforce each other (positive feedback) and which counteract each other (negative feedback). A quantified SD model translates this diagram into differential equations that can be simulated over time.
SD supports small-n evaluation in two ways. First, it provides a formal framework for mapping and testing a theory of change in feedback-rich systems. Many development interventions operate in systems where the outcome feeds back into the conditions that produced it. A microfinance programme increases household income, which increases repayment rates, which increases the loan fund, which enables more lending. An SD model makes these feedback structures explicit and testable. If the theory of change assumes a reinforcing loop but the SD model shows that a balancing loop (such as market saturation) dominates after a threshold, the evaluator has identified a structural flaw in the programme logic before collecting a single additional data point.
Second, SD enables scenario analysis for contribution analysis (Chapter 6). The central question in contribution analysis is whether the programme made a difference given all the other factors at play. An SD model can simulate two scenarios: one with the programme and one without. The difference between the two trajectories represents the programme’s simulated contribution. This is not a counterfactual in the experimental sense, because it depends on model assumptions. It is a structured counterfactual whose assumptions are transparent and open to scrutiny. Sterman (2000) provides the foundational treatment of SD modelling, including calibration, sensitivity testing, and validation procedures.






Development evaluation application: system dynamics for health system capacity planning



Homer & Hirsch (2006) developed a system dynamics model of the tuberculosis epidemic in India, modelling the feedback loops between detection, treatment, drug resistance, and transmission. The model was calibrated using epidemiological data from national health surveys. The evaluation team simulated two scenarios: one with an expanded DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course) programme and one without. The difference between the two trajectories estimated the programme’s contribution to case detection and mortality reduction over a 20-year horizon. The model identified a counterintuitive finding: expanding detection without adequate treatment capacity would increase drug-resistant TB through incomplete treatment courses, a negative feedback loop that verbal programme logic had not anticipated. The SD model disciplined the contribution analysis by revealing structural dynamics that cross-sectional comparison could not detect.

title: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs
text:
9.3 Integration with small-n methods
The value of simulation for small-n evaluation lies in its integration with the methods covered in earlier chapters. Table 9.1 summarises three concrete pairings.



Table 9.1: Integration protocols for simulation and small-n methods











Pairing
Simulation role
Practical protocol
Cross-reference




ABM + Bayesian process tracing
Generate prior and likelihood distributions
Build ABM of the causal mechanism; run Monte Carlo simulations; use output distributions as probability inputs for updating
Chapter 7

Construct and test the contribution story

ABM + QCA

The first pairing is the most developed in the literature. Befani et al. (2021) showed that an ABM of a health intervention could generate the sensitivity and specificity estimates needed for diagnostic tests, addressing the probability estimation challenge discussed in Chapter 7. The second pairing remains more conceptual. Several authors have used SD to map theories of change, but few have formally linked the SD output to a contribution analysis framework. The potential is clear: SD makes the contribution story dynamic and testable, but the integration protocol needs further methodological development. The third pairing is exploratory. QCA results depend on the cases included in the analysis, and an ABM can generate synthetic cases that test whether the solution holds under different parameter assumptions. This is analogous to a robustness check in statistical analysis, but implemented through simulation rather than through alternative model specifications.

title: 9  Computational Simulation as Complement to Small-n Designs
text:
9.4 Strengths, limitations, and applicability
Simulation handles complexity, non-linearity, and emergence in ways that verbal theories of change cannot. An ABM can reveal unintended consequences that arise from agent interactions. An SD model can identify tipping points and feedback traps. Both force the evaluator to make causal assumptions explicit, which disciplines the reasoning process even when the model itself is imperfect.
The limitations are equally clear. Model validity is always contestable. Every simulation rests on assumptions about agent rules (ABM) or functional forms (SD), and different assumptions can produce different conclusions. The risk of confirmation bias applies: an analyst who believes in the programme’s effectiveness may unconsciously calibrate the model to confirm that belief. Computational expertise is a practical barrier. Building and validating a simulation model requires skills that most evaluation teams do not possess, and the cost of acquiring those skills may not be justified for every evaluation. Finally, simulation results are model-dependent, not data-dependent. They complement empirical evidence; they do not substitute for it.



Table 9.2: Applicability of simulation approaches in small-n evaluation










Criterion

The case against and for computational simulation



Three criticisms recur. First, simulation models can produce any result by adjusting their assumptions. A model is only as good as its rules, and with enough degrees of freedom, the analyst can fit any narrative. Second, simulation adds a layer of abstraction that distances the evaluation from the empirical evidence. Stakeholders may distrust findings that rest on “what-if” scenarios rather than on observed data. Third, the expertise and time required to build credible simulations may not be proportionate to the evaluation budget, especially in resource-constrained settings.
Proponents respond on each point. On assumption sensitivity, pattern-oriented modelling and systematic sensitivity analysis constrain the parameter space and make the analyst’s choices transparent. A well-documented simulation is more transparent than a verbal theory of change, because every assumption is explicit and testable. On abstraction, simulation does not replace empirical evidence. It structures the evaluator’s reasoning about how the evidence fits together, and it fills specific gaps (probability estimates, counterfactual scenarios) that empirical methods cannot address in small-n settings. On expertise, the barrier is real but declining. User-friendly platforms (NetLogo for ABM, Vensim for SD) have lowered the technical threshold, and the growing availability of training resources is expanding the pool of evaluators who can use these tools competently.


Part II has surveyed six of the eight logics and their associated methods, from counterfactual designs through participatory approaches to computational simulation. Each logic addresses a different aspect of the causal question, and each has distinctive strengths and limitations. Part III turns to integration: how to combine these logics into coherent multi-method evaluation designs, how to select the right logic for a given evaluation problem, and how to maintain quality standards across the methodological repertoire.

title: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection
text:
10.1 Existing selection tools
Part II surveyed six of the eight logics and their associated methods. A reader who worked through those chapters now faces a practical question: given a specific evaluation problem, which logic applies? The methods landscape has expanded considerably in the past decade, and practitioners confront more options than ever. Without a principled selection procedure, the risk is that method choice is driven by the evaluator’s training rather than by the evaluation question. Within the evidence-to-policy ecosystem, a transparent decision procedure also strengthens the structures and processes pathway through which evidence influences policy (Sempé et al., 2025): commissioners can audit the logic of method selection, and evaluators can justify their design choices against a common frame of reference rather than relying on professional convention.
Two existing frameworks offer partial guidance. Fox et al. (2022) organises methods into two families: “effects of causes” (counterfactual designs) and “causes of effects” (theory-based approaches). This distinction is useful as a first cut, but it groups very different logics under the same heading. Process tracing, contribution analysis, and QCA all fall under “causes of effects,” yet they rest on different causal logics and answer different questions. White & Phillips (2012) takes a different approach, distinguishing quantitative approaches adaptable to small samples from qualitative and theory-based approaches. The 3ie framework also maps sources of bias and proposes common steps for causal analysis. Both tools advance the field. Neither maps the full set of evaluation questions to the expanded set of logics that this book covers.
The framework proposed here starts from the evaluation question rather than from the method. The analyst identifies the type of causal question being asked, characterises the programme attributes that constrain the design, and uses a decision matrix to arrive at a shortlist of logics and candidate methods.

title: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection
text:
10.2 A logic-based decision matrix
Table 10.1 maps six types of evaluation question against four programme attributes. Each cell identifies the logic or logics best suited to that combination and the candidate methods that implement them. The matrix is not prescriptive. Many evaluations will involve more than one question type, and the next chapter discusses how to combine logics within a single evaluation design.



Table 10.1: Decision matrix: evaluation questions, programme attributes, and recommended logics












Evaluation question
Few units (n < 5), rich data
Few units, limited data
Moderate n (5–30), heterogeneous contexts
Repeated measures on same units




Net effect: did the intervention cause the outcome?
Counterfactual: SCED, synthetic control
Counterfactual: randomisation inference with permutation tests
Counterfactual: SCED with replication across units
Counterfactual: SCED (reversal, multiple baseline)


Contribution: did the intervention contribute to the outcome alongside other factors?
Generative: contribution analysis, process tracing
Generative: contribution analysis with minimal ToC
Generative: contribution analysis across sites
Generative: contribution analysis with time-series ToC


Mechanism: how did the intervention produce the outcome?
Generative + Diagnostic: process tracing with Bayesian updating
Generative: process tracing (theory-testing variant)
Generative: realist evaluation (CMO configurations across sites)
Diagnostic: Bayesian process tracing with ABM-generated priors


Pathway diversity: which combinations of conditions produce the outcome?
Configurational: CNA (no frequency threshold)
Not feasible at very small n with limited data
Configurational: QCA (crisp or fuzzy set)
Configurational: within-case temporal QCA


Emergent outcomes: what unexpected changes occurred?
Participatory: outcome harvesting
Participatory: most significant change
Participatory: outcome harvesting across sites
Participatory: MSC with longitudinal story collection


System dynamics: how do feedback loops and non-linearities shape the outcome?
Simulation: SD model of the theory of change
Simulation: qualitative causal loop diagram
Simulation: ABM calibrated across sites
Simulation: SD with time-series calibration






Three features of the matrix deserve comment. First, the counterfactual logic applies more broadly than many practitioners assume. SCEDs and randomisation inference can operate with very few units, provided that repeated measurement is possible. The common belief that counterfactual designs require large samples reflects the dominance of the RCT model rather than a logical necessity. Second, the configurational logic has a genuine lower bound. QCA requires enough cases to distinguish necessary from sufficient conditions, and with fewer than five units the truth table is too sparse to support reliable inference. CNA has a lower threshold but still needs variation across cases. Third, simulation appears in the bottom row not as a standalone logic but as a complementary tool. It enters the matrix when the evaluation question involves feedback, non-linearity, or emergence, and it always operates alongside one of the empirical logics.
crumbs: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection

title: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection
text:
10.3 Worked examples
Three published evaluations illustrate how the matrix maps real evaluation problems to logics and methods.
Example A: Gender-responsive budgeting in Kabale District, Uganda. Bamanyaki & Holvoet (2016) evaluated a civil society initiative that advocated for gender-responsive budgeting to improve maternal health service delivery in a single Ugandan district. There was one treated unit (the district), rich qualitative data (advocacy records, budget documents, key informant interviews with local officials and health workers), and a complex causal chain from civil society advocacy through budget allocation to service delivery. The evaluation question spanned contribution and mechanism. Entering the matrix at “contribution, few units, rich data” points to contribution analysis and process tracing. Entering at “mechanism, few units, rich data” points to process tracing with Bayesian updating. The evaluators used exactly this combination: they mapped the programme theory linking advocacy to maternal health outcomes, identified the causal mechanism, derived observable predictions, and tested the evidence using Bayesian logic (hoop and smoking-gun tests). The evaluation design combined three logics: generative (contribution analysis mapped the theory of change), diagnostic (Bayesian tests assessed the strength of evidence for each mechanism step), and the process-tracing method bridged both. Persson et al. (2013), analysing anti-corruption reforms as a collective action problem, illustrate the same structural challenge: complex governance interventions with a single treated unit that demand theory-based causal reasoning rather than cross-case comparison.
Example B: NHS modernisation in London. Greenhalgh et al. (2009) evaluated the NHS Modernisation Agency’s “whole-scale transformation” programme across four health care organisations and three clinical areas (stroke, kidney, and sexual health), yielding roughly twelve site-service configurations with substantial contextual heterogeneity. The evaluation questions were about mechanism (how did the modernisation programme produce its outcomes?) and pathway diversity (why did it succeed in some configurations and not others?). Entering the matrix at “mechanism, moderate n, heterogeneous contexts” points to realist evaluation. The evaluators used precisely this: they developed initial context-mechanism-outcome (CMO) configurations, tested them against evidence from each site, and refined them iteratively. They found that modernisation succeeded where staff were engaged as active participants in change rather than passive recipients, and that the same programme inputs triggered different mechanisms depending on organisational history, leadership support, and prior relationships. The matrix would additionally recommend configurational logic (QCA) to systematically identify which combinations of contextual conditions were jointly sufficient for success — a design enhancement that would have complemented the realist findings with formal set-theoretic analysis. Blackman et al. (2011), applying systems-thinking approaches to public health evaluation across English local authorities, illustrate the complementary configurational perspective.
Example C: Writing intervention for struggling first-graders in Spain. Arrimada et al. (2018) evaluated a Response to Intervention (RTI) writing programme for twelve first-grade students who scored below the 15th percentile in composition. Each student was measured on handwriting quality and composition through twice-weekly probe tasks across approximately 192 days. The evaluation questions were about net effect (did the intervention cause improvement in writing quality?) and dose-response (did adding text-planning instruction produce gains beyond transcription training alone?). Entering the matrix at “net effect, repeated measures” points to SCED, specifically a multiple-baseline design staggered across participants. The evaluators used exactly this: baseline measurement continued for 120 days of regular classroom instruction, followed by 72 days of parent-delivered intervention staggered across students. The staggered introduction ruled out maturation and history as rival explanations. Ten of twelve students improved in composition quality, with eight reaching the normal range. Horner et al. (2005) provide the methodological foundation for this approach, reviewing how single-subject research designs generate evidence-based practice in educational settings.
crumbs: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection

title: 10  A Decision Framework for Method Selection
text:
10.4 When to combine logics
The worked examples illustrate a pattern. Every realistic evaluation scenario involves more than one question type, and no single logic answers all of them. The decision matrix helps the evaluator identify which logics are needed, but it does not specify how to combine them. Sequential, concurrent, and nested integration designs each have different strengths and different coordination requirements. The next chapter addresses these integration protocols directly.
