title: 11  Mixed-Method Integration Across Logics
text:
11.1 Patterns of integration in the literature
Single-logic evaluations answer one type of causal question. A SCED estimates whether the intervention changed the outcome. A process trace explains how. A QCA identifies which conditions produce the outcome. Most evaluation commissioners need answers across these question types, and that requires combining logics. This chapter examines how the six logics covered in Part II are combined in practice, identifies three integration designs, and proposes quality criteria for multi-logic evaluation.
Before prescribing integration protocols, it is worth asking which logics are actually combined in the published literature. Figure 11.1 presents a co-occurrence analysis of evaluation method terms in publications indexed by OpenAlex between 2000 and 2025. Each cell reports the number of publications that mention methods from both logics.
Three patterns stand out. First, the configurational–generative pairing dominates. Qualitative comparative analysis and process tracing co-occur in over 500 publications, reflecting the well-established “nested analysis” tradition in comparative politics and policy research. QCA identifies configurations associated with the outcome; process tracing investigates the mechanisms within selected cases.
Second, simulation methods co-occur with nearly every other logic. System dynamics and agent-based modelling appear alongside configurational, generative, and participatory methods, consistent with this book’s argument that simulation is a complementary tool rather than a standalone logic (Chapter 9). Third, counterfactual methods are strikingly isolated. SCEDs, N-of-1 trials, and randomisation inference rarely co-occur with methods from other logics. The counterfactual tradition has developed largely in parallel with the theory-based and configurational traditions. This isolation is a gap, not a feature. The decision matrix in the previous chapter (Chapter 10) suggests several productive pairings that the literature has not yet explored in depth, including SCED with process tracing and randomisation inference with QCA.

title: 11  Mixed-Method Integration Across Logics
text:
Figure 11.1: Cross-logic co-occurrence in the evaluation literature, 2000–2025. Cell values show the number of publications mentioning method terms from both logics. Data: OpenAlex, queried March 2026.






Lieberman (2005) formalised the nested analysis strategy, combining large-n statistical analysis with small-n case studies selected on the basis of the statistical results. Schneider & Rohlfing (2013) extend this to QCA, proposing formal protocols for combining set-theoretic analysis with process tracing.

title: 11  Mixed-Method Integration Across Logics
text:
11.2 Integration designs
Three design types structure the combination of logics within a single evaluation.

11.2.1 Sequential integration
In a sequential design, one logic precedes and informs the other. The most common pattern is configurational logic first, then generative logic. A QCA across fifteen programme sites identifies three configurations associated with improved outcomes. The evaluator then selects one or two cases from each configuration for process tracing, testing whether the mechanisms posited by the theory of change actually operate in those contexts. The ordering matters: the configurational analysis constrains case selection, ensuring that the process traces are not drawn from convenient cases but from analytically informative ones. The reverse sequence is also possible. An evaluator conducts process tracing in a small number of cases, identifies candidate mechanisms, and then uses QCA across the full set of cases to test whether those mechanisms are associated with the outcome under specific conditions.


11.2.2 Concurrent integration
In a concurrent design, two or more logics operate in parallel on the same cases during the same evaluation period. A behaviour-change pilot uses SCEDs to estimate the intervention effect for each participant while simultaneously collecting Most Significant Change stories at regular intervals. The counterfactual logic provides an effect estimate; the participatory logic captures outcomes that the quantitative measures miss. Integration happens at the interpretation stage. The evaluator examines whether the MSC stories align with the SCED effect estimates, and whether the stories reveal mechanisms or contextual factors that explain variation across participants.


11.2.3 Nested integration
In a nested design, one logic is embedded within another. A contribution analysis maps the overall contribution story for a governance reform programme. Within that contribution story, specific mechanism steps are assessed using Bayesian process tracing with diagnostic tests. The generative logic provides the structural framework; the diagnostic logic provides the evidential rigour for critical causal claims within that framework. The simulation logic can be nested within the diagnostic logic in turn: an agent-based model generates the probability estimates that the diagnostic tests require (Chapter 9).


Befani & Mayne (2014) demonstrate how process tracing and contribution analysis can be formally combined in a nested design, with diagnostic tests providing the evidential foundation for critical links within the contribution story.
Table 11.1 summarises the three types.



Table 11.1: Three integration designs for multi-logic evaluation











Design
Sequencing
When to use
Coordination challenge




Sequential
Logic A precedes and informs Logic B
When one logic constrains case selection or hypothesis formation for another
Timing: Logic A must be complete before Logic B begins


Concurrent
Logics A and B operate in parallel
When different logics address different questions on the same cases
Interpretation: findings must be synthesised across logics


Nested
Logic B is embedded within Logic A
When one logic provides the framework and another provides evidential depth at specific points
Coherence: the embedded logic must be compatible with the framing logic

title: 11  Mixed-Method Integration Across Logics
text:
11.3 Quality criteria for integration
Three criteria distinguish rigorous multi-logic integration from ad hoc method mixing.
The first criterion is coherence. The combined logics must rest on compatible assumptions. Generative and diagnostic logics combine naturally: both operate within a mechanistic ontology, and the diagnostic logic provides a formal test for the evidence that the generative logic identifies. Configurational and generative logics also combine well: QCA identifies patterns, and process tracing explains them. Some pairings are less natural. QCA’s deterministic set-theoretic logic sits uneasily with Bayesian probabilistic updating. The evaluator who combines these must be explicit about which logic governs which claim and must avoid treating configurational necessity claims as probability estimates.
The second criterion is transparency. The integration rationale must be reported explicitly. A multi-logic evaluation report should state which question each logic addresses, why that logic was chosen, and how the findings from different logics are brought together. Too many mixed-method evaluations combine methods without explaining the logic of combination. The result is a collection of findings rather than an integrated causal account.
The third criterion is the treatment of divergence. When logics disagree, the disagreement is informative, not a failure. A process trace may support a mechanism that a QCA does not find to be necessary. This divergence suggests that the mechanism operates but is not the only path to the outcome. A SCED may show no effect while an MSC story reports meaningful change. This divergence suggests that the quantitative measure is not capturing what changed, or that the change is real but not attributable to the intervention. The evaluator should report divergence openly and use it to refine the causal account rather than suppress it.

title: 11  Mixed-Method Integration Across Logics
text:
11.4 Looking ahead
This chapter and the preceding one have addressed method selection and method combination. The next chapter turns to a different dimension of quality: biases, transparency, and reporting standards that apply across all small-n methods.

Lieberman, E. S. (2005). Nested analysis as a mixed-method strategy for comparative research. American Political Science Review, 99(3), 435–452.

title: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards
text:
12.1 Sources of bias at small n
Every evaluation method is vulnerable to bias. At large n, a biased observation is diluted by the rest of the sample. At small n, each case, each interview, each data point carries more inferential weight, and a single biased input can shift the conclusion. The quality challenge in small-n evaluation is not that bias is more frequent, but that it is less forgiving. This chapter maps the main sources of bias, proposes mitigation strategies, and reviews reporting standards that apply across the logics covered in this book.
White & Phillips (2012) identified three families of bias that affect small-n impact evaluations. Each operates differently depending on the logic being used, but all three are present across methods.
Respondent biases arise when the people providing evidence distort it, whether deliberately or unconsciously. Social desirability bias leads respondents to report what they think the evaluator wants to hear. Recall bias distorts accounts of past events, especially when participants are asked to reconstruct causal sequences after the fact. Attribution inflation leads stakeholders to over-attribute observed changes to the programme, either because they want the programme to continue or because the programme is the most salient recent event in their experience. These biases are most acute for participatory methods (Chapter 8), where the causal evidence depends on stakeholder testimony, but they also affect process tracing when key informant interviews form the primary evidence base.
Evaluator biases arise from the analyst’s own reasoning processes. Confirmation bias leads evaluators to seek and weight evidence that supports their prior beliefs about the programme’s effectiveness. In Bayesian process tracing (Chapter 7), Befani (2020) identified confirmation bias as a specific risk: evaluators may set priors too high or interpret ambiguous evidence as confirming the hypothesis. Anchoring bias leads evaluators to fix on early evidence and adjust insufficiently as new evidence arrives. The availability heuristic leads evaluators to overweight vivid or recent evidence relative to systematic but less memorable data. These biases are present across all logics but are hardest to detect in methods that rely on qualitative judgement, precisely because the judgement is exercised by the person who is also responsible for the evaluation’s conclusions.
Selection bias arises from the choice of cases, sites, or units. At small n, case selection is an analytical decision rather than a sampling procedure, and the wrong choice can predetermine the finding. Selecting on the dependent variable (choosing only cases where the programme succeeded) inflates the apparent effectiveness of the intervention. Convenience sampling (choosing cases that are easy to access) introduces systematic differences between the studied cases and the population of interest. In configurational methods (Chapter 5), the choice of cases determines the truth table and can affect which solutions the analysis identifies.
crumbs: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards

title: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards
text:
12.2 Mitigation strategies
Four strategies reduce the risk of bias in small-n evaluations, though none eliminates it entirely.
Structured protocols constrain the evaluator’s discretion at each step. A pre-specified theory of change, agreed before data collection begins, defines which mechanisms the evaluator will investigate and which evidence would confirm or disconfirm each one. Coding rubrics specify how evidence is classified. Checklists ensure that the evaluator considers rival explanations systematically rather than selectively.
Pre-registration of causal claims is borrowed from the experimental tradition but applies equally to theory-based methods. The evaluator states, before collecting data, what causal claims the evaluation will test and what evidence would support or undermine each claim. Pre-registration does not prevent bias, but it makes bias visible. A reader can compare the pre-registered claims with the final report and assess whether the evaluator shifted the goalposts.
Blinding separates evidence collection from causal assessment where feasible. In Bayesian process tracing, one team member can gather and code evidence while another sets the prior probabilities and conducts the updating, reducing the risk that the person interpreting the evidence is influenced by knowing what conclusion it supports. Blinding is more difficult in participatory methods, where the evaluator is often directly involved in facilitating the evidence-generation process.
Sensitivity analysis varies the analyst’s assumptions and reports whether the conclusions change. The specific form of sensitivity analysis depends on the logic being used, and each method family has developed its own protocols.
In QCA, sensitivity analysis involves testing whether the solution holds when cases are added or removed, when calibration thresholds are shifted (particularly the crossover point), or when conditions are recoded. Schneider & Wagemann (2012) recommend varying the crossover point by at least one standard deviation in each direction and reporting whether the truth table rows change and whether the minimised solution is altered. If a single case’s movement across the crossover point changes the solution, that case is pivotal, and the finding is fragile.
In Bayesian process tracing, sensitivity analysis involves varying the prior probability and the sensitivity and false-positive rate estimates for each test, and checking whether the posterior odds still support the same conclusion. Befani (2020) suggests computing posterior probabilities under “optimistic” (high sensitivity, low false-positive rate) and “pessimistic” (low sensitivity, high false-positive rate) scenarios. If the conclusion holds under both, the finding is robust to probability estimation uncertainty. If the conclusion reverses under the pessimistic scenario, the evaluation should report that the causal claim is sensitive to the analyst’s probability judgements.
In contribution analysis, sensitivity analysis involves considering alternative contribution stories and assessing whether the evidence distinguishes between them. Each causal link in the theory of change should be tested against the most plausible rival explanation for that link, not just the overall alternative hypothesis. If the contribution story rests on a single causal link for which the evidence is ambiguous, the evaluation should report that link as a point of fragility.
In SCED analysis, sensitivity analysis involves testing whether the effect estimate changes when different analytical methods (visual inspection, LMEM, NAP, Tau-U) are applied, when outlier data points are removed, or when the phase boundaries are shifted by one or two observations. Kratochwill et al. (2013) recommend that SCED studies report results under multiple analytical approaches and note whether the conclusions align.
If the conclusion is sensitive to reasonable alternative assumptions under any of these protocols, the evaluator should report that fragility rather than suppress it.
crumbs: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards

title: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards
text:
12.3 Reporting standards
Several method-specific reporting standards exist. SCRIBE (Single-Case Reporting guideline In BEhavioural interventions) provides a structured checklist for SCED studies (Tate et al., 2016). The CONSORT extension for N-of-1 trials (CENT) adapts the familiar clinical trial reporting framework to single-case designs (Shamseer et al., 2015). RAMESES (Realist And Meta-narrative Evidence Syntheses: Evolving Standards) covers realist evaluation and realist synthesis (Wong et al., 2013). Transparency standards for QCA specify how to report calibration decisions, truth table analyses, and solution terms (Schneider & Wagemann, 2010).
These standards share common elements: they require the evaluator to report design decisions, describe data sources, justify analytical choices, and acknowledge limitations. They differ in what they emphasise, reflecting the different logics on which each method rests. No existing standard covers multi-logic evaluations or addresses the specific quality challenges of combining methods across logics.
Table 12.1 proposes a unified reporting checklist for small-n impact evaluations. The items are organised by evaluation phase and apply regardless of which logic or combination of logics the evaluation uses.



Table 12.1: Proposed reporting checklist for small-n impact evaluations









Phase
Reporting item




Design
State the evaluation questions and the logic(s) selected to address each one



Justify the choice of logic(s) with reference to programme attributes and data availability



Describe the case/unit selection procedure and the criteria used



Report any pre-registration of causal claims or analytical protocols


Data
Describe each data source and its relationship to the causal claim



Report potential biases for each data source (respondent, evaluator, selection)



Describe the steps taken to mitigate each identified bias


Analysis
Report the analytical procedure for each logic used



Describe calibration, coding, or classification decisions and their justification



Report sensitivity analyses and their results


Interpretation
State the causal claim(s) and the evidential warrant for each



Report convergence or divergence across logics, if a multi-logic design was used



Identify limitations that the mitigation strategies did not address



Distinguish between findings that are well-supported and findings that are tentative






The checklist is not a replacement for method-specific standards. Evaluators using SCEDs should follow SCRIBE in addition to this checklist. Evaluators using realist evaluation should follow RAMESES. The checklist fills a gap: it provides a common framework for evaluations that combine methods, and it ensures that readers can assess the quality of a small-n evaluation regardless of which tradition it comes from.
From an EIPM perspective, structured reporting standards serve as institutional infrastructure for evidence credibility (Sempé et al., 2025). Evidence users and intermediaries in the policy ecosystem rely on the transparency and rigour of evaluation reports when deciding how much weight to give causal findings. When small-n evaluations follow ad hoc reporting conventions, the causal claims they produce are harder for non-specialist audiences to appraise, reducing the likelihood that the evidence enters the policy process. The checklist above operationalises the structures and processes pathway by providing a standardised instrument through which evaluation quality can be assessed across logics.
The final chapter draws these threads together, arguing that small-n evaluation is not a concession to constraints but a distinct mode of causal inquiry with its own standards of rigour.

crumbs: 12  Bias, Transparency, and Quality Standards

title: 13  Small-n Evaluation as Causal Inquiry
text:
13.1 A research agenda
Development programmes routinely operate at scales that defeat standard statistical inference. A ministry reform has one treated unit. A district health pilot covers four sites. A training cohort enrols fifteen participants. The publication trends in Chapter 1 show that evaluators are responding: the volume of work on process tracing, QCA, realist evaluation, and single-case designs has grown sharply since 2010. But growth in volume has not been matched by growth in conceptual integration. Methods have developed within separate disciplinary traditions — clinical psychology for SCEDs, comparative politics for QCA, public health for realist evaluation — and practitioners still lack a shared vocabulary for explaining why they chose one approach over another and what kind of causal warrant it provides.
This book proposed a vocabulary. The eight logics of causal inference (Chapter 3) name the distinct ways in which an evaluator can justify a causal claim when cross-case statistical comparison is unavailable or insufficient. Six of those logics received dedicated treatment in Part II: counterfactual reasoning through within-unit temporal comparison (Chapter 4), multiple-causation reasoning through configurational methods (Chapter 5), generative reasoning through process tracing, contribution analysis, and realist evaluation (Chapter 6), diagnostic reasoning through Bayesian updating (Chapter 7), participatory reasoning through stakeholder-validated causal narratives (Chapter 8), and simulated reasoning through computational modelling (Chapter 9). Two logics — regularity and manipulation — were treated briefly because their small-n applicability is narrower, though both remain present as components of other methods.
The framework does not rank these logics. Each answers a different causal question. An anti-corruption evaluation that asks “did the reform reduce fraud?” needs counterfactual or diagnostic evidence. The same evaluation, reframed as “why did the reform work in three provinces but not in two?”, needs configurational evidence. Reframed again as “how did the reform change bureaucratic behaviour?”, it needs generative evidence. These are not competing answers to the same question. They are answers to different questions, and the decision matrix (Chapter 10) and integration protocols (Chapter 11) help the evaluator decide which questions to ask and how to combine the logics that answer them.
The framework also does not claim that small-n methods are interchangeable with large-n methods. They answer different questions. A cluster-randomised trial of a conditional cash transfer programme estimates the average treatment effect across hundreds of communities. A process trace in one community explains how the transfer changed household decision-making. A QCA across fifteen communities identifies which combinations of programme features and contextual conditions produced the outcome. These are not competing answers to the same question. They are answers to different questions, each valid on its own terms and more valuable in combination.
Four gaps deserve attention.
First, the field lacks formal frameworks for meta-inference: combining evidence generated by different logics into a coherent causal account. The integration chapter (Chapter 11) described sequential, concurrent, and nested designs, but these are procedural arrangements, not inferential frameworks. When a QCA result and a process-tracing finding point in different directions, how should the evaluator weight the two? When a SCED effect estimate is paired with an MSC story, what is the combined evidential warrant? These questions require formal development that goes beyond the scope of this book.
Second, software platforms for small-n evaluation remain fragmented. QCA has dedicated packages (fsQCA, the QCA package in R). Bayesian process tracing has prototype tools but no widely adopted platform. Agent-based modelling and system dynamics have their own environments (NetLogo, Vensim). No integrated platform allows an evaluator to move between logics within a single analytical workflow. Building such a platform would lower the practical barriers to multi-logic evaluation.
Third, systematic reviews of small-n impact evaluations in development are scarce. The co-occurrence analysis in Chapter 11 showed which methods are discussed together in the published literature, but it did not assess how well those methods are applied. Preliminary evidence from an evidence gap map of over 5,000 empirical studies in fragile and conflict-affected settings suggests that specialised small-n methods remain rare even in contexts where small samples are the norm: process tracing, QCA, contribution analysis, and Bayesian methods together accounted for fewer than 60 of the 5,327 studies examined (Sempé, 2026). How often do QCA studies in development evaluation follow established transparency standards? How often do process-tracing evaluations pre-register their causal claims? How often do participatory evaluations include independent substantiation? Answering these questions requires a systematic review that codes evaluations against the quality standards proposed in Chapter 12.
Fourth, evaluation training and curricula have not kept pace with the expansion of the methods repertoire. Most evaluation courses still organise methods by tradition (quantitative versus qualitative) rather than by causal logic. A logic-based curriculum would start with the question “what kind of causal claim do you want to make?” and work outwards to the methods that support that claim, rather than starting with the method and hoping the right question emerges.
These four gaps map directly onto the pathways of change identified in the EIPM framework (Sempé et al., 2025). Meta-inference frameworks and training reform address the capabilities pathway, strengthening evaluators’ and commissioners’ ability to generate and appraise multi-logic evidence. Integrated software platforms address the structures and processes pathway, embedding methodological rigour in the tools that evaluators use. Systematic reviews address the evidence culture pathway, establishing community standards for how small-n methods should be applied and evaluated. Progress on any one pathway reinforces the others, and advance on all four would move small-n evaluation from a specialist niche into the routine methodological repertoire of the evidence-to-policy ecosystem.
